title: Valkey: The Complete A–Z Guide
description: A comprehensive end-to-end guide to Valkey — from fundamentals to production. Covers installation (official + Percona repos), all data types and commands, persistence, replication, high availability (HA overview comparing Sentinel vs Cluster), Sentinel deployment (automatic failover), Cluster mode (horizontal scaling), security (ACL/TLS), performance tuning, Valkey 9.x built-in search, use cases (caching, sessions, rate limiting, queues, leaderboards), Docker deployment, monitoring with Prometheus/Grafana, backups, troubleshooting, and production best practices — for beginners and advanced users alike.
author: E-Learning Platform
heroAlt: Valkey architecture diagram showing data types (Strings, Lists, Sets, Sorted Sets, Hashes, Streams), production infrastructure with master-replica-Sentinel-Cluster, and use cases including caching, sessions, rate limiting, and queues

sections.title: Introduction to Valkey
sections.description: What Valkey is, its history, and why it is the most popular community-driven in-memory data store.

items.label: What is Valkey?
items.text:
Valkey is a high-performance, open-source, in-memory key-value data store that was created in March 2024 as a community-driven fork of Valkey OSS (version 7.2.4). The fork occurred after Valkey Ltd. shifted its licensing from the permissive BSD 3-Clause license to a dual-license structure (SSPL/RSAL), which restricted certain commercial uses.

Valkey operates under the BSD 3-Clause license and is governed by the Linux Foundation — ensuring it remains fully open-source and free. Major contributors include AWS, Google Cloud, Oracle, and Ericsson. Valkey is designed as a drop-in replacement for Valkey OSS, meaning applications written against Valkey 7.2.x require minimal to no code changes.

As of early 2026, Valkey has diverged from Valkey with its own innovations:
• Valkey 8.x — Enhanced I/O multithreading model with near-linear scaling on multi-core systems, delivering ~8-10% higher throughput and ~20% better P99 latency; optimized memory efficiency and improved hashtables
• Valkey 9.x — Full-text and hybrid search, vector similarity search, advanced retrieval capabilities, built-in search without external modules

Valkey 8.x+ also introduces advanced observability features including **per-slot metrics** that provide deep insight into performance bottlenecks — far beyond standard Valkey monitoring. The project has been widely adopted by major cloud providers (AWS ElastiCache, Google Cloud Memorystore) and high-scale tech firms.

items.label: Architecture Overview
items.text:
Before touching configurations, understand the layers of a typical Valkey deployment. Your application interacts with Valkey through a client library (like ioredis for Node.js, redis-py for Python, or predis for PHP), which manages connection pooling and command execution.

```text
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    YOUR APPLICATION                      │
│             (Node.js / Python / PHP / Go)                │
└──────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘
                           │  Client Library (ioredis / redis-py)
                           │  (Connection Pooling & Retries)
┌──────────────────────────▼───────────────────────────────┐
│                VALKEY SERVER :6379                       │
│  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  │
│  │    ACL       │  │    Auth      │  │  Persistence   │  │
│  │   Users      │  │   Password   │  │   RDB + AOF    │  │
│  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └────────────────┘  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Security Principle:** Access to the Valkey port should be denied to everyone except trusted clients. In a production network, Valkey instances should only be directly accessible by the application servers that use them.

sections.title: Installation & Setup
sections.description: Installing Valkey on Ubuntu/Debian via official APT, Percona repository, and Docker.

items.label: Install Valkey via Official APT Repository
items.code:
# Method 1: Install from official Valkey APT repository
# Requires Ubuntu 24.04+ or Debian 12+
user@server:~$ sudo apt update
user@server:~$ sudo apt install valkey -y

# Optional: Install compatibility symlinks for redis-* CLI tools
user@server:~$ sudo apt install valkey-redis-compat -y

user@server:~$ valkey-server --version
Valkey server v=9.1.8 sha=00000000:0 malloc=jemalloc-5.3.0 bits=64 build=def456

user@server:~$ valkey-cli ping
PONG

items.label: Install Valkey via Percona Repository (Method 1)
items.code:
# Method 2a: Install via Percona repository (wget + lsb-release)
# Recommended for older or non-Ubuntu 24.04+ distributions

# Install prerequisites
user@server:~$ sudo apt install wget lsb-release gnupg -y

# Download and add the Percona repository (distribution-specific)
user@server:~$ wget https://repo.percona.com/apt/percona-release_latest.$(lsb_release -sc)_all.deb
user@server:~$ sudo dpkg -i percona-release_latest.$(lsb_release -sc)_all.deb

# Enable the Valkey experimental repository
user@server:~$ sudo percona-release enable valkey experimental

# Install Valkey
user@server:~$ sudo apt update
user@server:~$ sudo apt install valkey -y

# Verify the installation
user@server:~$ valkey-server --version
user@server:~$ valkey-cli ping
PONG

items.label: Install Valkey via Percona Repository (Method 2)
items.code:
# Method 2b: Install via Percona repository (generic .deb)
# Works on any Debian-based distribution regardless of release name

# Download the generic Percona release package
user@server:~$ wget https://repo.percona.com/apt/percona-release_latest.generic_all.deb

# Install the package
user@server:~$ sudo apt install ./percona-release_latest.generic_all.deb -y

# Enable the Valkey experimental repository
user@server:~$ sudo percona-release enable valkey experimental

# Install Valkey
user@server:~$ sudo apt update
user@server:~$ sudo apt install valkey -y

# Verify
user@server:~$ valkey-cli ping
PONG

items.label: Install Valkey via Percona Repository (Method 3 — with dpkg fix)
items.code:
# Method 2c: Install via Percona (with curl + dpkg fix)
# Use this method if you encounter repository/dpkg issues with Methods 2a/2b

# Install prerequisites
user@server:~$ sudo apt install curl -y

# Fix any existing dpkg issues
user@server:~$ sudo dpkg --configure -a
user@server:~$ sudo apt install -f

# Add Percona repository and enable Valkey
user@server:~$ sudo percona-release enable valkey experimental

# Install Valkey
user@server:~$ sudo apt update
user@server:~$ sudo apt install valkey -y

# Verify
user@server:~$ valkey-server --version
user@server:~$ valkey-cli ping
PONG

items.label: Valkey with Docker (Recommended for Development)
items.code:
# Pull the latest Valkey image
user@server:~$ docker pull valkey/valkey:9-alpine

# Basic single instance
user@server:~$ docker run -d --name my-valkey -p 6379:6379 valkey/valkey:9-alpine

# With persistence and config
user@server:~$ docker run -d --name my-valkey \
  -p 6379:6379 \
  -v valkey-data:/data \
  -v $(pwd)/valkey.conf:/etc/valkey/valkey.conf \
  valkey/valkey:9-alpine \
  valkey-server /etc/valkey/valkey.conf

# Test connectivity
user@server:~$ docker exec -it my-valkey valkey-cli ping
PONG

items.label: Connecting with valkey-cli
items.code:
# Connect to local default
user@server:~$ valkey-cli
127.0.0.1:6379> ping
PONG
127.0.0.1:6379> info server
# Server
valkey_version:9.1.8
valkey_mode:standalone
os:Linux
arch_bits:64

# Connect to remote server
user@server:~$ valkey-cli -h 192.168.1.100 -p 6379 -a 'your-password'

# Connect via Unix socket
user@server:~$ valkey-cli -s /var/run/valkey/valkey-server.sock

# Run a single command
user@server:~$ valkey-cli GET mykey
"myvalue"

# Monitor all live commands
user@server:~$ valkey-cli monitor

items.label: Service Management with systemd
items.code:
# Start / Stop / Restart / Status
user@server:~$ sudo systemctl start valkey
user@server:~$ sudo systemctl stop valkey
user@server:~$ sudo systemctl restart valkey
user@server:~$ sudo systemctl status valkey

# Enable auto-start on boot
user@server:~$ sudo systemctl enable valkey

# Check logs in real-time
user@server:~$ sudo journalctl -u valkey -f

items.label: Valkey Configuration File Reference
items.code:
# The main configuration file is located at:
#   /etc/valkey/valkey.conf
#
# Edit it with your preferred editor:
user@server:~$ sudo nano /etc/valkey/valkey.conf

# --- Network ---
bind 127.0.0.1          # Listen on localhost only (secure default)
port 6379               # Default port
protected-mode yes      # Rejects connections unless auth is set

# --- Authentication ---
requirepass YourStrongPassword123

# --- Memory ---
maxmemory 512mb                   # Set a hard limit (ALWAYS do this)
maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru      # Evict LRU keys when full

# --- Persistence ---
save 900 1          # Save if 1 key changed in 900 seconds
save 300 10         # Save if 10 keys changed in 300 seconds
save 60 10000       # Save if 10000 keys changed in 60 seconds
appendonly yes      # Enable AOF (append-only file) for durability
appendfsync everysec  # Flush to disk every second (safe + fast)

# --- Performance (Valkey 9.x multi-threaded) ---
io-threads 4        # Set to number of CPU cores to dedicate
io-threads-do-reads yes

# --- Logging ---
loglevel notice
logfile /var/log/valkey/valkey.log

# After editing, restart Valkey:
user@server:~$ sudo systemctl restart valkey

sections.title: Core Data Types & Commands
sections.description: Every data type in Valkey — Strings, Lists, Sets, Sorted Sets, Hashes, Streams, Bitmaps, HyperLogLog, and Geospatial — with all commands and practical examples.

items.label: Strings — The Foundation
items.text:
Strings are the most basic Valkey data type. They can store any sequence of bytes — text, integers, floats, serialized JSON, binary data (images), up to 512 MB per key. Strings are the foundation for caching, counters, and simple key-value stores.

Key commands: SET, GET, GETSET, MSET, MGET, INCR, INCRBY, DECR, DECRBY, APPEND, STRLEN, SETNX, SETEX, GETDEL, GETEX, SUBSTR.

items.label: String Commands in Action
items.code:
# Basic set/get
127.0.0.1:6379> SET user:1:name "Alice"
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> GET user:1:name
"Alice"

# Set with expiration (N seconds)
127.0.0.1:6379> SETEX session:token:abc123 3600 "user_data_here"
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> TTL session:token:abc123
(integer) 3598

# Set only if key does not exist (SETNX)
127.0.0.1:6379> SETNX lock:resource:42 "locked"
(integer) 1   # Key was set
127.0.0.1:6379> SETNX lock:resource:42 "locked"
(integer) 0   # Key already exists

# Counters (atomic increment/decrement)
127.0.0.1:6379> SET page:home:views 0
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> INCR page:home:views
(integer) 1
127.0.0.1:6379> INCRBY page:home:views 100
(integer) 101
127.0.0.1:6379> DECR page:home:views
(integer) 100

# Multi-get/set (batch operations)
127.0.0.1:6379> MSET user:1:name "Alice" user:1:email "alice@example.com" user:1:age "30"
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> MGET user:1:name user:1:email user:1:age
1) "Alice"
2) "alice@example.com"
3) "30"

# Get and delete / get and set
127.0.0.1:6379> SET temp "temporary_value"
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> GETDEL temp
"temporary_value"
127.0.0.1:6379> GET temp
(nil)

# Append to existing value
127.0.0.1:6379> SET log "Error: "
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> APPEND log "connection timeout"
(integer) 23
127.0.0.1:6379> GET log
"Error: connection timeout"

items.label: Lists — Doubly Linked Lists
items.text:
Lists are ordered collections of strings, implemented as doubly linked lists. They excel at queue-like operations — pushing to one end and popping from the other. Perfect for message queues, activity feeds, and time-series data.

Key commands: LPUSH, RPUSH, LPOP, RPOP, LLEN, LRANGE, LINDEX, LSET, LREM, LTRIM, BLPOP, BRPOP, RPOPLPUSH, BRPOPLPUSH.

items.label: List Commands in Action
items.code:
# Building a queue
127.0.0.1:6379> RPUSH tasks "Process order 123"
(integer) 1
127.0.0.1:6379> RPUSH tasks "Send email receipt" "Update inventory" "Generate invoice"
(integer) 4

127.0.0.1:6379> LRANGE tasks 0 -1
1) "Process order 123"
2) "Send email receipt"
3) "Update inventory"
4) "Generate invoice"

# Process from front of queue
127.0.0.1:6379> LPOP tasks
"Process order 123"
127.0.0.1:6379> LLEN tasks
(integer) 3

# Blocking pop — waits for an element (timeout: 0 = forever)
127.0.0.1:6379> BLPOP task_queue 5
(nil)  # Timed out after 5 seconds

# Activity feed (recent items first)
127.0.0.1:6379> LPUSH feed:user:42 "John liked your post"
127.0.0.1:6379> LPUSH feed:user:42 "Sarah commented on your photo"
127.0.0.1:6379> LPUSH feed:user:42 "New follower: Mike"
127.0.0.1:6379> LTRIM feed:user:42 0 9   # Keep only latest 10
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> LRANGE feed:user:42 0 -1
1) "New follower: Mike"
2) "Sarah commented on your photo"
3) "John liked your post"

# Get element by index
127.0.0.1:6379> LINDEX feed:user:42 0
"New follower: Mike"

items.label: Sets — Unordered Unique Collections
items.text:
Sets are unordered collections of unique strings. They excel at membership testing, deduplication, and set operations like intersection, union, and difference. Perfect for tags, user groups, and unique visitors tracking.

Key commands: SADD, SREM, SISMEMBER, SMEMBERS, SCARD, SPOP, SRANDMEMBER, SINTER, SUNION, SDIFF, SSCAN, SMISMEMBER.

items.label: Set Commands in Action
items.code:
# Tag management
127.0.0.1:6379> SADD post:42:tags "redis" "database" "tutorial" "performance"
(integer) 4
127.0.0.1:6379> SADD post:42:tags "database"  # Duplicate — ignored
(integer) 0

127.0.0.1:6379> SMEMBERS post:42:tags
1) "database"
2) "tutorial"
3) "redis"
4) "performance"

127.0.0.1:6379> SISMEMBER post:42:tags "redis"
(integer) 1
127.0.0.1:6379> SISMEMBER post:42:tags "nosql"
(integer) 0

# Set operations — find related content
127.0.0.1:6379> SADD post:1:tags "redis" "database" "caching"
127.0.0.1:6379> SADD post:2:tags "redis" "performance" "scaling"
127.0.0.1:6379> SADD post:3:tags "database" "postgresql" "sql"

# Posts that share tags with post:42 (intersection)
127.0.0.1:6379> SINTER post:42:tags post:1:tags
1) "redis"
2) "database"

# All unique tags (union)
127.0.0.1:6379> SUNION post:42:tags post:1:tags post:2:tags
1) "database"
2) "tutorial"
3) "redis"
4) "performance"
5) "caching"
6) "scaling"

# Tags only in post:42 (difference)
127.0.0.1:6379> SDIFF post:42:tags post:1:tags
1) "tutorial"
2) "performance"

# Random member (for sampling)
127.0.0.1:6379> SRANDMEMBER post:42:tags 2
1) "redis"
2) "tutorial"

# Remove and return random member
127.0.0.1:6379> SPOP post:42:tags
"performance"

items.label: Sorted Sets — Ordered Unique Collections
items.text:
Sorted Sets (ZSETs) are like Sets, but each member has an associated floating-point score. Members are ordered by score, enabling range queries, leaderboards, priority queues, and autocomplete. They are among the most powerful Valkey data structures.

Key commands: ZADD, ZRANGE, ZREVRANGE, ZSCORE, ZINCRBY, ZCARD, ZCOUNT, ZREM, ZRANK, ZREVRANK, ZRANGEBYSCORE, ZREVRANGEBYSCORE, ZREMRANGEBYSCORE, ZREMRANGEBYRANK, ZINTERSTORE, ZUNIONSTORE, ZPOPMAX, ZPOPMIN, BZPOPMAX, BZPOPMIN, ZDIFF, ZMSCORE.

items.label: Sorted Set Commands in Action
items.code:
# Leaderboard — game scores
127.0.0.1:6379> ZADD leaderboard:game1 1500 "player:alice"
(integer) 1
127.0.0.1:6379> ZADD leaderboard:game1 2200 "player:bob"
127.0.0.1:6379> ZADD leaderboard:game1 1800 "player:charlie"
127.0.0.1:6379> ZADD leaderboard:game1 3100 "player:diana"
127.0.0.1:6379> ZADD leaderboard:game1 950 "player:eve"

# Top 3 players (highest to lowest)
127.0.0.1:6379> ZREVRANGE leaderboard:game1 0 2 WITHSCORES
1) "player:diana"
2) "3100"
3) "player:bob"
4) "2200"
5) "player:charlie"
6) "1800"

# Bottom 3 players (lowest to highest)
127.0.0.1:6379> ZRANGE leaderboard:game1 0 2 WITHSCORES
1) "player:eve"
2) "950"
3) "player:alice"
4) "1500"
5) "player:charlie"
6) "1800"

# Get player rank (0-based)
127.0.0.1:6379> ZREVRANK leaderboard:game1 "player:bob"
(integer) 1  # Second place
127.0.0.1:6379> ZSCORE leaderboard:game1 "player:bob"
"2200"

# Increment score (player scored!)
127.0.0.1:6379> ZINCRBY leaderboard:game1 500 "player:alice"
"2000"
127.0.0.1:6379> ZREVRANK leaderboard:game1 "player:alice"
(integer) 2  # Moved up from 4th to 3rd!

# Players with scores between 1500 and 3000
127.0.0.1:6379> ZRANGEBYSCORE leaderboard:game1 1500 3000 WITHSCORES
1) "player:alice"
2) "2000"
3) "player:charlie"
4) "1800"
5) "player:bob"
6) "2200"

# Priority queue (lower score = higher priority)
127.0.0.1:6379> ZADD priority_queue 3 "job:send-email"
127.0.0.1:6379> ZADD priority_queue 1 "job:process-payment"
127.0.0.1:6379> ZADD priority_queue 5 "job:generate-report"

# Pop highest priority (lowest score)
127.0.0.1:6379> ZPOPMIN priority_queue
1) "job:process-payment"
2) "1"

items.label: Hashes — Objects & Records
items.text:
Hashes are maps between string fields and string values, perfect for representing objects. Instead of storing a user as a serialized JSON string (which requires reading and rewriting the entire object on every update), hashes let you modify individual fields independently.

Key commands: HSET, HGET, HGETALL, HMGET, HMSET, HDEL, HEXISTS, HLEN, HKEYS, HVALS, HINCRBY, HSTRLEN, HSCAN, HSETNX, HRANDFIELD.

items.label: Hash Commands in Action
items.code:
# User profile as a hash
127.0.0.1:6379> HSET user:1000 name "Alice Johnson" email "alice@example.com" age 30 city "New York"
(integer) 4

127.0.0.1:6379> HGET user:1000 name
"Alice Johnson"
127.0.0.1:6379> HGETALL user:1000
1) "name"
2) "Alice Johnson"
3) "email"
4) "alice@example.com"
5) "age"
6) "30"
7) "city"
8) "New York"

# Get specific fields only
127.0.0.1:6379> HMGET user:1000 name email city
1) "Alice Johnson"
2) "alice@example.com"
3) "New York"

# Increment a field atomically
127.0.0.1:6379> HINCRBY user:1000 login_count 1
(integer) 1
127.0.0.1:6379> HINCRBY user:1000 login_count 1
(integer) 2

# Check if field exists
127.0.0.1:6379> HEXISTS user:1000 phone
(integer) 0
127.0.0.1:6379> HEXISTS user:1000 email
(integer) 1

# Get all keys or values
127.0.0.1:6379> HKEYS user:1000
1) "name"
2) "email"
3) "age"
4) "city"
5) "login_count"

127.0.0.1:6379> HVALS user:1000
1) "Alice Johnson"
2) "alice@example.com"
3) "30"
4) "New York"
5) "2"

# Delete a field
127.0.0.1:6379> HDEL user:1000 city
(integer) 1

# Hash size
127.0.0.1:6379> HLEN user:1000
(integer) 4

items.label: Streams — Event Log & Message Queue
items.text:
Streams are append-only log data structures that store messages ordered by time. They are Valkey's answer to Apache Kafka — ideal for event sourcing, activity tracking, message queues, and real-time data pipelines. Each stream entry has a unique ID (timestamp-sequence) and a set of field-value pairs.

Key commands: XADD, XREAD, XRANGE, XREVRANGE, XLEN, XDEL, XTRIM, XGROUP, XREADGROUP, XACK, XCLAIM, XINFO, XPENDING.

items.label: Stream Commands in Action
items.code:
# Add events to a stream
127.0.0.1:6379> XADD events:orders * user_id 42 order_id 1001 amount 49.99
"1728547200000-0"  # Auto-generated ID: timestamp-sequence

127.0.0.1:6379> XADD events:orders * user_id 42 order_id 1002 amount 129.99
"1728547200123-0"

127.0.0.1:6379> XADD events:orders * user_id 55 order_id 1003 amount 19.99
"1728547200456-0"

# Read all entries
127.0.0.1:6379> XRANGE events:orders - +  # '-' = start, '+' = end
1) 1) "1728547200000-0"
   2) 1) "user_id"
      2) "42"
      3) "order_id"
      4) "1001"
      5) "amount"
      6) "49.99"
2) 1) "1728547200123-0"
   2) ...

# Read new entries only (like Kafka consumer)
127.0.0.1:6379> XREAD COUNT 10 BLOCK 0 STREAMS events:orders $
# ^ '$' means "only new entries from now"

# Consumer groups — for distributing work
127.0.0.1:6379> XGROUP CREATE events:orders email-workers $ MKSTREAM
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> XGROUP CREATE events:orders notification-workers $ MKSTREAM
OK

# Read as a consumer group (each message goes to one consumer)
127.0.0.1:6379> XREADGROUP GROUP email-workers worker-1 COUNT 1 BLOCK 2000 STREAMS events:orders >

# Acknowledge processed messages
127.0.0.1:6379> XACK events:orders email-workers "1728547200000-0"
(integer) 1

# Trim stream to keep only the latest 1000 entries
127.0.0.1:6379> XTRIM events:orders MAXLEN ~ 1000
OK

# Check pending messages (unacknowledged)
127.0.0.1:6379> XPENDING events:orders email-workers
1) (integer) 0  # No pending messages

items.label: Bitmaps — Space-Efficient Booleans
items.text:
Bitmaps are not a separate data type — they are String operations on bits. Each string can store up to 2^32 bits (512 MB), allowing you to track boolean state for billions of items using minimal memory. Perfect for feature flags, user sign-ins, and A/B testing.

Key commands: SETBIT, GETBIT, BITCOUNT, BITPOS, BITOP, BITFIELD.

Example: Track daily active users — each user ID maps to a bit position. SETBIT dau:2026-06-08 42 1 marks user 42 as active on that day. BITCOUNT dau:2026-06-08 counts total daily active users.

items.label: Bitmap & HyperLogLog Examples
items.code:
# Bitmap: Daily active users tracking
# Each bit position = user ID
127.0.0.1:6379> SETBIT dau:2026-06-08 42 1     # User 42 visited
(integer) 0
127.0.0.1:6379> SETBIT dau:2026-06-08 100 1    # User 100 visited
(integer) 0
127.0.0.1:6379> SETBIT dau:2026-06-08 42 0     # Mark as not active
(integer) 1

127.0.0.1:6379> GETBIT dau:2026-06-08 42        # Is user 42 active?
(integer) 0
127.0.0.1:6379> BITCOUNT dau:2026-06-08          # Total DAU count
(integer) 1

# HyperLogLog: Unique visitor counting (approximate)
# Uses ~12 KB per key, error rate ~0.81% — no matter how many items!
127.0.0.1:6379> PFADD unique:visitors:june "192.168.1.1" "10.0.0.5" "192.168.1.1" "172.16.0.8"
(integer) 1
127.0.0.1:6379> PFCOUNT unique:visitors:june
(integer) 3  # Duplicates automatically handled

# Merge multiple HyperLogLogs
127.0.0.1:6379> PFADD unique:visitors:july "10.0.0.5" "203.0.113.42"
127.0.0.1:6379> PFMERGE unique:visitors:summer unique:visitors:june unique:visitors:july
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> PFCOUNT unique:visitors:summer
(integer) 4  # Combined unique visitors across both months

items.label: Geospatial — Location-Based Queries
items.text:
Valkey geospatial commands let you store longitude/latitude coordinates and query by distance, radius, and bounding box. Uses the sorted set data type internally with a geohash as the score.

Key commands: GEOADD, GEODIST, GEORADIUS, GEORADIUSBYMEMBER, GEOPOS, GEOHASH, GEOSEARCH.

items.label: Geospatial & Key Operations
items.code:
# Store locations
127.0.0.1:6379> GEOADD locations 13.361389 38.115556 "Palermo" 15.087269 37.502669 "Catania"
(integer) 2

# Distance between two points (km)
127.0.0.1:6379> GEODIST locations Palermo Catania km
"166.2742"

# Find locations within 200km radius of Palermo
127.0.0.1:6379> GEORADIUS locations 13.361389 38.115556 200 km
1) "Palermo"
2) "Catania"

# With distance and coordinates
127.0.0.1:6379> GEORADIUS locations 13.361389 38.115556 200 km WITHDIST WITHCOORD
1) 1) "Palermo"
   2) "0.0000"
   3) 1) "13.361389338970184"
      2) "38.115556395496299"
2) 1) "Catania"
   2) "166.2742"
   3) 1) "15.087267458438873"
      2) "37.502668423331621"

items.label: Pub/Sub — Publish-Subscribe Messaging
items.text: Valkey supports Publish/Subscribe messaging, where publishers send messages to channels and subscribers receive messages in real-time. This is ideal for real-time notifications, chat systems, event broadcasting, and distributed system coordination.
items.code:
# Terminal 1 — Subscribe to a channel
127.0.0.1:6379> SUBSCRIBE mychannel
Reading messages... (press Ctrl-C to quit)
1) "subscribe"
2) "mychannel"
3) (integer) 1

# Terminal 2 — Publish a message
127.0.0.1:6379> PUBLISH mychannel "Hello from Valkey!"
(integer) 1

# Subscribe to multiple channels with pattern matching
127.0.0.1:6379> PSUBSCRIBE news.*
1) "psubscribe"
2) "news.*"
3) (integer) 1

# Publish to a matching channel
127.0.0.1:6379> PUBLISH news.tech "New Valkey 9.2 released!"
(integer) 1

items.label: Key Expiration & TTL
items.text:
Valkey supports automatic key expiration with sub-millisecond precision. This is essential for caching, session management, and any time-bound data.

Commands: EXPIRE, PEXPIRE, EXPIREAT, TTL, PTTL, PERSIST, SETEX, PSETEX.

items.label: TTL & Key Management Commands
items.code:
# Set expiration on existing key
127.0.0.1:6379> SET cache:weather "sunny, 24°C"
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> EXPIRE cache:weather 300    # Expire in 5 minutes
(integer) 1
127.0.0.1:6379> TTL cache:weather
(integer) 297

# Set with expiration in one command
127.0.0.1:6379> SETEX session:abc123 86400 "user_data"   # 24 hours
OK

# Remove expiration (make persistent)
127.0.0.1:6379> PERSIST session:abc123
(integer) 1
127.0.0.1:6379> TTL session:abc123
(integer) -1  # No expiration

# Check if key exists
127.0.0.1:6379> EXISTS cache:weather
(integer) 1
127.0.0.1:6379> EXISTS non_existent_key
(integer) 0

# Get key type
127.0.0.1:6379> TYPE user:1000
hash
127.0.0.1:6379> TYPE leaderboard:game1
zset

# Delete keys
127.0.0.1:6379> DEL cache:weather
(integer) 1
127.0.0.1:6379> DEL user:1:name user:1:email user:1:age
(integer) 3

# Scan keys (NEVER use KEYS * in production!)
127.0.0.1:6379> SCAN 0 MATCH user:* COUNT 100
1) "23"  # Next cursor
2) 1) "user:1000"
   2) "user:1:name"
   3) "user:1:email"
   ...

sections.title: Transactions, Pipelining & Lua Scripting
sections.description: Ensuring atomicity, reducing network round trips, and running server-side logic in Valkey.

items.label: Transactions with MULTI/EXEC
items.text:
Valkey transactions use MULTI/EXEC/DISCARD to batch commands and guarantee atomic execution. All commands in a transaction are queued and executed sequentially — no other commands can interleave. However, Valkey transactions do NOT support rollback. If a command fails syntactically, it's caught before execution. If it fails at runtime (e.g., applying an operation to the wrong data type), the error is returned but other commands in the transaction still execute.

WATCH provides optimistic locking — if a watched key changes before the transaction executes, the entire transaction is aborted (returns nil).

items.label: Transaction Examples
items.code:
# Basic transaction — atomic bank transfer
127.0.0.1:6379> MULTI
OK
127.0.0.1:6379(TX)> DECRBY account:alice 100
QUEUED
127.0.0.1:6379(TX)> INCRBY account:bob 100
QUEUED
127.0.0.1:6379(TX)> EXEC
1) (integer) 50   # Alice's new balance
2) (integer) 150  # Bob's new balance

# Optimistic locking with WATCH
127.0.0.1:6379> WATCH account:alice
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> GET account:alice
"150"
127.0.0.1:6379> MULTI
OK
127.0.0.1:6379(TX)> DECRBY account:alice 100
QUEUED
127.0.0.1:6379(TX)> INCRBY account:bob 100
QUEUED
127.0.0.1:6379(TX)> EXEC
(nil)  # Transaction aborted! Someone modified account:alice between WATCH and EXEC

# Discard transaction
127.0.0.1:6379> MULTI
OK
127.0.0.1:6379(TX)> SET key1 "value1"
QUEUED
127.0.0.1:6379(TX)> SET key2 "value2"
QUEUED
127.0.0.1:6379(TX)> DISCARD
OK

items.label: Pipelining — Batch Commands for Performance
items.text:
Pipelining sends multiple commands to the server without waiting for each reply. Instead of N round trips (each with network latency), you send all commands at once and read all replies together. This can improve throughput by 5-10× on high-latency networks.

Pipelining is NOT transactional — commands are still interleaved with other clients. Use MULTI/EXEC for atomicity, pipelining for throughput.

items.label: Pipelining in Different Languages
Lua Scripting with EVAL
items.text:
Lua scripting allows you to execute arbitrary Lua code on the Valkey server. Scripts are atomic — no other commands run while your script is executing. This is the most powerful way to implement complex multi-key operations, custom data structures, and server-side logic.

Scripts can be cached with SCRIPT LOAD and executed by SHA1 hash with EVALSHA, eliminating network transfer of the script on every call.

items.label: Lua Script Examples
items.code:
# EVAL script with arguments
# KEYS[1] = key, ARGV[1] = limit, ARGV[2] = increment
127.0.0.1:6379> EVAL "
    local current = redis.call('GET', KEYS[1]) or 0
    local limit = tonumber(ARGV[1])
    if tonumber(current) >= limit then
        return 0  -- Rate limit exceeded
    end
    redis.call('INCR', KEYS[1])
    redis.call('EXPIRE', KEYS[1], ARGV[2])
    return 1  -- Allowed
" 1 rate:limit:user:42 100 60

# Compare-and-swap (CAS) operation
127.0.0.1:6379> EVAL "
    local current = redis.call('GET', KEYS[1])
    if current == ARGV[1] then
        redis.call('SET', KEYS[1], ARGV[2])
        return 1
    end
    return 0
" 1 account:alice "150" "200"

# Load and execute by hash (faster)
127.0.0.1:6379> SCRIPT LOAD "return redis.call('GET', KEYS[1])"
"4e6d..."  # SHA1 hash
127.0.0.1:6379> EVALSHA "4e6d..." 1 user:1:name
"Alice"

# Built-in Lua library functions available:
# redis.call() - Run Valkey command, raises error on failure
# redis.pcall() - Same but catches errors, returns error object
# redis.log() - Write to Valkey log file
# All standard Lua 5.1 libraries (string, table, math, etc.)

sections.title: Persistence: RDB, AOF & Hybrid
sections.description: How Valkey saves data to disk — tradeoffs between durability, performance, and recovery time.

items.label: RDB — Point-in-Time Snapshots
items.text:
RDB (Valkey Database) performs full snapshots of your dataset at configurable intervals. The snapshot is a compressed binary file (dump.rdb) that can be used for backups, disaster recovery, or seeding new replicas.

Pros: Fast recovery (load the entire dataset at once). Very compact on disk. Ideal for backups and cold-start scenarios.

Cons: Data loss window between snapshots (up to your save interval). Not suitable as the sole persistence mechanism for critical data.

Configuration in valkey.conf:
save 900 1    # Save if at least 1 key changed in 900 seconds
save 300 10   # Save if at least 10 keys changed in 300 seconds
save 60 10000 # Save if at least 10000 keys changed in 60 seconds

items.label: AOF — Append-Only File
items.text:
AOF logs every write operation as a Valkey protocol command. On restart, Valkey replays all commands to rebuild the dataset. AOF is more durable than RDB (configurable fsync policy) but produces larger files and is slower to replay.

fsync policies:
always — fsync on every write (safest, ~50% throughput reduction)
everysec — fsync once per second (good balance, ~1 second data loss) — Recommended
no — Let OS decide (fastest, highest data loss potential)

AOF files can be rewritten to compact them (BGREWRITEAOF) — the process creates a new AOF with only the minimum commands needed to represent the current dataset.

items.label: Hybrid Persistence (Recommended in Valkey 7+)
items.code:
# valkey.conf — Hybrid persistence configuration
# =============================================

# Enable both RDB and AOF (recommended for production)
# Valkey loads AOF at restart (AOF is more complete), then uses RDB for
# faster initial loading by embedding an RDB preamble in the AOF file.

# RDB Configuration
save 900 1
save 300 10
save 60 10000
stop-writes-on-bgsave-error yes
rdbcompression yes
rdbchecksum yes
dbfilename dump.rdb

# AOF Configuration
appendonly yes                    # Enable AOF
appendfilename "appendonly.aof"
appendfsync everysec              # fsync once per second
no-appendfsync-on-rewrite no
auto-aof-rewrite-percentage 100   # Rewrite when AOF doubles in size
auto-aof-rewrite-min-size 64mb    # Minimum size before rewriting
aof-load-truncated yes
aof-use-rdb-preamble yes          # Hybrid: RDB preamble + AOF tail

# View persistence info
127.0.0.1:6379> INFO persistence
# Persistence
loading:0
rdb_changes_since_last_save:0
rdb_bgsave_in_progress:0
rdb_last_save_time:1728547200
rdb_last_bgsave_status:ok
rdb_last_bgsave_time_sec:3
rdb_current_bgsave_time_sec:-1
aof_enabled:1
aof_rewrite_in_progress:0
aof_rewrite_scheduled:0
aof_last_rewrite_time_sec:2
aof_current_size:1048576
aof_base_size:524288
aof_pending_rewrite:0
aof_last_bgrewrite_status:ok

items.label: Manual Backup & Restore
items.code:
# Manual RDB snapshot (non-blocking)
127.0.0.1:6379> BGSAVE
Background saving started

# Blocking save (not recommended in production — blocks all clients)
127.0.0.1:6379> SAVE
OK

# Location of RDB file
127.0.0.1:6379> CONFIG GET dir
1) "dir"
2) "/var/lib/valkey"
127.0.0.1:6379> CONFIG GET dbfilename
1) "dbfilename"
2) "dump.rdb"

# Manual AOF rewrite
127.0.0.1:6379> BGREWRITEAOF
Background append only file rewriting started

# Backup script (recommended)
user@server:~$ cat /usr/local/bin/backup-valkey.sh
#!/bin/bash
BACKUP_DIR="/backups/valkey"
DATE=$(date +%Y-%m-%d_%H%M%S)

# Trigger RDB then copy it
valkey-cli BGSAVE
sleep 2
cp /var/lib/valkey/dump.rdb $BACKUP_DIR/valkey_$DATE.rdb

# Copy AOF as additional safety
cp /var/lib/valkey/appendonly.aof $BACKUP_DIR/valkey_aof_$DATE.aof

# Compress
gzip $BACKUP_DIR/valkey_$DATE.rdb

# Keep only last 7 days of backups
find $BACKUP_DIR -name "*.rdb.gz" -mtime +7 -delete

# Restore procedure:
# 1. Stop Valkey: sudo systemctl stop valkey
# 2. Copy dump.rdb to /var/lib/valkey/
# 3. Start Valkey: sudo systemctl start valkey

rows: No persistence, all lost on restart

sections.title: Replication
sections.description: Master-replica replication — how Valkey replicates data, replication internals, and best practices.

items.label: How Valkey Replication Works
items.text:
Valkey replication is asynchronous and follows a master-replica (formerly master-slave) model. A master can have multiple replicas. Replicas maintain an exact copy of the master's dataset and can accept read-only queries.

Replication process:
1. Replica connects to master and issues PSYNC (partial sync) or FULLRESYNC
2. If partial sync is possible, master sends buffered commands from the replication backlog
3. If not (new replica, backlog too old), master forks a child process, creates an RDB snapshot, and sends it to the replica
4. Replica loads the RDB, then replays any buffered commands
5. After initial sync, master continuously streams write commands to replicas (replication ID + offset tracking)

Replication is single-threaded on the master (one connection per replica) and asynchronous — the master does NOT wait for replicas to acknowledge writes.

items.label: Configuring Replication
items.code:
# On replica server — edit valkey.conf:
# =======================================

replicaof 192.168.1.10 6379    # Master IP and port
masterauth your-master-password  # If master has requirepass
replica-serve-stale-data yes    # Serve old data during sync
replica-read-only yes           # Replicas are read-only (default)
repl-diskless-sync yes         # Master sends RDB over socket (no temp file)
repl-diskless-sync-delay 5     # Wait 5s for more replicas to sync together
repl-backlog-size 100mb        # Replication backlog (larger = more tolerance for disconnects)
repl-backlog-ttl 3600          # How long to keep backlog when no replicas connected
replica-priority 100           # Lower = higher priority for Sentinel promotion
min-replicas-to-write 1        # Master stops writes if < 1 replica connected
min-replicas-max-lag 10        # Max lag in seconds before considering replica disconnected

# Verify replication status
127.0.0.1:6379> INFO replication
# Replication
role:master
connected_slaves:2
slave0:ip=192.168.1.20,port=6379,state=online,offset=123456,lag=0
slave1:ip=192.168.1.21,port=6379,state=online,offset=123456,lag=0
master_replid:abc123...
master_replid2:def456...
master_repl_offset:123456
second_repl_offset:-1
repl_backlog_active:1
repl_backlog_size:104857600
repl_backlog_first_byte_offset:1
repl_backlog_histlen:123456

items.label: Configuring Replication with ACLs (Production Method)
items.text:
In production, don't use the simple requirepass method. Replicas authenticate as the default user, so the default user must be granted specific replication permissions in the ACL file.

**Master ACL File (`/etc/valkey/users.acl`):**
```text
# Default user: Restricted but allowed to sync data
user default on >ReplicaPass123! +psync +replconf +ping -@dangerous

# App user: Your application uses this for data operations
user appuser on >AppPass@2024! ~* +@read +@write -@dangerous
```

**Replica `valkey.conf`:**
```ini
bind 0.0.0.0
port 6379
replicaof 192.168.68.200 6379
# MUST match the password assigned to 'default' in the ACL file
masterauth ReplicaPass123!
```

Key takeaway: `masterauth` is the password the replica presents when authenticating as the `default` user. The `+psync` permission on the default user is what allows replication to proceed.

items.label: Replication Best Practices
text: 1. Use replicas for read scaling — offload read queries to replicas (caching, reporting, analytics)
2. NEVER write to a replica — replicas should be read-only unless using Valkey 7+ replica writes (advanced use case)

3. Set repl-backlog-size appropriately — the backlog allows replicas to resume partial sync after short disconnects. A good rule is: backlog_size = avg_connection_reconnect_time × peak_write_throughput
4. Monitor replication lag — use INFO replication and alert if lag exceeds 10 seconds
5. Use at least 2 replicas for production — provides redundancy if one replica fails
6. Chain replication (replica → sub-replica) — can reduce master load but increases replication lag
7. Consider replicas for backups — run BGSAVE on a replica to avoid impacting the master's performance
8. Test failover scenarios — simulate a master failure and verify the replica promotes correctly

sections.title: High Availability Overview: Sentinel vs Cluster
sections.description: Understanding the two approaches to Valkey high availability — Sentinel (automatic failover) and Cluster (horizontal scaling with sharding) — and when to use each.

items.label: Two Paths to High Availability
items.text: Valkey offers **two distinct methods** for achieving high availability. Choosing the right one depends on your dataset size, scaling needs, and operational complexity tolerance.

rows: Automatic failover when master goes down
Data is sharded across nodes (16,384 hash slots)
Lower — simpler to set up and operate
Moderate datasets, simpler ops, existing apps
Large datasets, high write volume, elastic scaling

items.label: When to Use Sentinel vs Cluster
text: Use Sentinel mode when:
• Your entire dataset fits comfortably in a single server's RAM (e.g., < 64 GB)
• You want automatic failover with minimal operational complexity
• Your application needs full support for multi-key operations and transactions
• You're migrating from a standalone Valkey instance and want high availability with minimal changes
• You need Pub/Sub, Lua scripting, and other single-node features without restrictions
Use Cluster mode when:
• Your dataset exceeds the RAM of a single server (e.g., > 64 GB and growing)
• You need to scale write throughput horizontally across multiple nodes
• You can design your data model around hash-tag-based key grouping
• Your client library supports cluster mode (MOVED/ASK redirects)
• You need both high availability AND elastic scalability together

Can you combine both? No — they are mutually exclusive. Sentinel manages failover for a master-replica setup. Cluster has its own built-in failover mechanism using gossip protocol and node voting. You don't run Sentinel on top of a Cluster.

items.label: Quick Decision Flowchart
items.code:
Is your dataset larger than a single server's RAM?
├── YES → Use Cluster Mode (or consider caching/CDN)
└── NO  → Do you need automatic failover?
        ├── YES → Use Sentinel Mode
        └── NO  → Standalone + Replication is enough

sections.title: Method 1 — Sentinel: Automatic Failover
sections.description: How Valkey Sentinel provides automatic failover, monitoring, and notification for master-replica deployments.

items.label: What is Valkey Sentinel?
items.text:
Valkey Sentinel is a distributed system that provides high availability for Valkey. At minimum, you need 3 Sentinel processes (odd number for quorum) that monitor your Valkey instances and perform automatic failover if the master becomes unavailable.

Sentinel capabilities:
• Monitoring — Constantly checks if master and replicas are working
• Notification — Alerts system administrators via API when something goes wrong
• Automatic failover — If a master fails, Sentinel promotes a replica to master and reconfigures other replicas to follow the new master
• Configuration provider — Clients can query Sentinel for the current master address

Sentinel uses a quorum (majority) to make decisions. With 3 Sentinels, you need at least 2 to agree that the master is down before initiating a failover.

items.label: 3-VM Configuration Breakdown
items.text:
When deploying Valkey Sentinel across 3 virtual machines, you need to decide which VM acts as the **master** and which as **replicas**. The table below shows the exact breakdown of which configuration file goes on which VM. The same  is used on all three nodes.

**Key rules:**
• **Master VM** (VM 1, 192.168.68.200):  — NO `replicaof` line. Includes `masterauth` so it can authenticate if it later becomes a replica after a failover.
• **Replica VMs** (VM 2, 192.168.68.201 & VM 3, 192.168.68.202):  — MUST have `replicaof 192.168.68.200 6379` pointing to the master.
• **All 3 VMs**: Identical  — Sentinel runs on every node.
• **Authentication**: Use the same `requirepass` and `masterauth` password on all nodes (`nepo123` in this example).
• **Quorum**: Set to 2 (majority of 3 Sentinels).

items.label: Sentinel Configuration
items.code:
# /etc/valkey/sentinel.conf — Run on 3 separate nodes
# ====================================================

port 26379
daemonize no
pidfile /var/run/valkey-sentinel.pid
logfile /var/log/valkey/sentinel.log

# Monitor the master
# sentinel monitor <name> <ip> <port> <quorum>
sentinel monitor mymaster 192.168.1.10 6379 2

# Sentinel will wait this many milliseconds to consider the master down
sentinel down-after-milliseconds mymaster 5000

# Number of replicas to reconfigure in parallel during failover
sentinel parallel-syncs mymaster 1

# Failover timeout
sentinel failover-timeout mymaster 10000

# Auth (if master requires password)
sentinel auth-pass mymaster your-master-password

# Notification script (called on failover events)
sentinel notification-script mymaster /usr/local/bin/sentinel-notify.sh

# Start Sentinel
user@server:~$ valkey-sentinel /etc/valkey/sentinel.conf

# Or via systemd
user@server:~$ sudo systemctl start valkey-sentinel@mymaster

# Verify Sentinel status
user@server:~$ valkey-cli -p 26379 SENTINEL master mymaster
1) "name"
2) "mymaster"
3) "ip"
4) "192.168.1.10"
5) "port"
6) "6379"
7) "runid"
8) "abc..."
9) "flags"
10) "master"
...

user@server:~$ valkey-cli -p 26379 SENTINEL replicas mymaster
1) 1) "name"
   2) "192.168.1.20:6379"
   3) "flags"
   4) "slave"
   ...

user@server:~$ valkey-cli -p 26379 SENTINEL get-master-addr-by-name mymaster
1) "192.168.1.10"
2) "6379"

items.label: Sentinel Failover Process
items.text:
When a master fails:

1. Detection — Each Sentinel with flags the master as subjectively down (SDOWN) if it doesn't receive a reply to PING within down-after-milliseconds

2. Verification — The Sentinel that detected SDOWN asks other Sentinels if the master is reachable. If the quorum (e.g., 2 of 3) agree, the master is marked as objectively down (ODOWN)

3. Election — Sentinels vote for a leader Sentinel to coordinate the failover. The leader is chosen via the Raft consensus algorithm

4. Failover — The leader Sentinel:
   • Picks the best replica (highest replication offset, lowest priority, lowest runid)
   • Reconfigures the replica to become the new master (REPLICAOF NO ONE)
   • Reconfigures other replicas to follow the new master
   • Updates the configuration so Sentinels monitor the new layout
   • Notifies clients via the notification script

5. Recovery — When the old master comes back online, it detects the new master and automatically configures itself as a replica

sections.title: Method 1 — Sentinel: Production Deployment Walkthrough
sections.description: A complete real-world walkthrough of building a production-ready Sentinel-based HA cluster — covering topology, configuration files, troubleshooting real issues, and verification commands.

items.label: Architecture Topology
items.text:
This deployment uses a three-node topology with three Sentinel instances to provide automatic failover. All nodes live on the same private subnet (192.168.68.0/24) and use shared global authentication.

Node Layout:
- Master: 192.168.68.200:6379
- Replica 1: 192.168.68.201:6379
- Replica 2: 192.168.68.202:6379 (ready for deployment)
- Sentinel 1-3: 192.168.68.200-202:26379

Global Authentication:
  Database password (requirepass): nepo123
  Inter-node replication password (masterauth): nepo123

Sentinel Quorum: 2 (out of 3 Sentinels must agree the master is down before failover initiates)

items.label: Master Node Configuration
items.text: The Master server acts as the primary read/write entry point. It has no `replicaof` line initially but includes `masterauth` so it can cleanly authenticate if it ever reboots as a replica after a failover event.
items.code:
# /etc/valkey/valkey.conf on 192.168.68.200 — Master Node

# Network & Security
bind 0.0.0.0
protected-mode yes
port 6379
tcp-backlog 511
timeout 0
tcp-keepalive 300

# Process & Logging
daemonize no
pidfile /var/run/valkey_6379.pid
loglevel notice
logfile ""
databases 16

# Snapshotting & Storage
stop-writes-on-bgsave-error yes
rdbcompression yes
rdbchecksum yes
dbfilename dump.rdb
dir /var/lib/valkey

# Replication Settings
replica-serve-stale-data yes
replica-read-only yes
repl-diskless-sync yes
repl-diskless-sync-delay 5
repl-backlog-size 100mb
repl-backlog-ttl 3600
replica-priority 100

# Authentication:
requirepass nepo123
masterauth nepo123

items.label: Replica Node Configuration
items.text: Replicas mirror the master in real-time and are explicitly locked to read-only mode to prevent application data divergence.
items.code:
# /etc/valkey/valkey.conf on 192.168.68.201 & 192.168.68.202 — Replica Nodes

# Network & Security (Matches Master)
bind 0.0.0.0
protected-mode yes
port 6379

daemonize no
pidfile /var/run/valkey_6379.pid
dir /var/lib/valkey

# Active Replication Target
replicaof 192.168.68.200 6379

# Replication Settings
replica-serve-stale-data yes
replica-read-only yes
repl-diskless-sync yes
repl-diskless-sync-delay 5
repl-backlog-size 100mb

# Authentication
requirepass nepo123
masterauth nepo123

items.label: Sentinel Configuration
items.text: The Sentinel processes monitor the cluster layout and handle automated master promotion during crashes. This file must be identical across all three nodes.
items.code:
# /etc/valkey/sentinel.conf — Identical on all three nodes

port 26379
protected-mode no
daemonize no
pidfile /var/run/valkey-sentinel.pid
dir /var/lib/valkey

# Monitor Target (Name, IP, Port, Quorum)
sentinel monitor mymaster 192.168.68.200 6379 2
sentinel auth-pass mymaster nepo123

# Failover Timers
sentinel down-after-milliseconds mymaster 10000
sentinel failover-timeout mymaster 30000
sentinel parallel-syncs mymaster 1
sentinel deny-scripts-reconfig yes

items.label: Troubleshooting Real Deployment Issues
items.text:
During this deployment several issues were encountered and resolved. These are the exact steps taken to get the cluster healthy.

1. Read-Only Verification
Confirmed replica status via `valkey-cli INFO replication`, locating the `slave_read_only:1` key to verify replicas are properly locked to read-only.

2. Sentinel Immediate Crashes (status=1/FAILURE)
Sentinel strictly aborts if a `sentinel notification-script` path is declared in the configuration file but the physical script file does not exist or lacks executable permissions. Resolved by stripping out the empty script declaration line.

3. File System Permissions
Addressed file system blockages by enforcing ownership: `sudo chown -R valkey:valkey /etc/valkey /var/lib/valkey`.

4. Ghost Node Connection State (127.0.0.1 vs Real IP)
Discovered Sentinel rewriting itself to 127.0.0.1 due to default values being applied on first run. Solved by stopping the service, resetting raw text parameters to target 192.168.68.200, and flushing memory cache.

5. Replica Communication Isolation (s_down and master-link-status: err)
A major cluster block was caused by `min-replicas-to-write 1` being active during the initial setup phase when replicas had not yet synced. Commented out the threshold variable to allow successful initial handshakes.

6. Relative Path Issues
Replaced relative path designations (`dir ./`) with strict absolute path models (`dir /var/lib/valkey`) to bypass permissions limits.

items.label: Verification & Status Commands
items.code:
# Check master cluster parameters from any Sentinel node
user@server:~$ valkey-cli -p 26379 SENTINEL master mymaster
# Expected: flags: master, num-slaves: 1+

# Check real-time replication stream conditions from replicas
user@server:~$ valkey-cli -p 26379 SENTINEL replicas mymaster
# Expected: flags: slave, master-link-status: ok, master-host: 192.168.68.200

# Force Sentinel to re-scan and discard stale cached node definitions
user@server:~$ valkey-cli -p 26379 SENTINEL RESET mymaster

sections.title: Method 2 — Cluster: Horizontal Scaling & Sharding
sections.description: Sharding data across multiple Valkey nodes with built-in failover — for datasets that exceed a single server's RAM.

items.label: What is Valkey Cluster?
items.text:
Valkey Cluster provides automatic sharding (partitioning) across multiple Valkey nodes, enabling you to store datasets larger than the RAM of a single machine. Data is automatically distributed across nodes using hash slots (16,384 total slots), and the cluster provides a degree of availability through automatic failover.

Key characteristics:
• Automatic sharding — Every key is mapped to one of 16,384 slots using CRC16(key) % 16384
• Decentralized — No proxy or single point of failure. All nodes communicate via gossip protocol (cluster bus, port +10000)
• Partial availability — If a node fails, only the slots it owns become unavailable (unless replicas exist)
• Smart clients — Clients connect to any node and are redirected to the correct node (MOVED redirect) or given a proxy-like cluster topology (ASK redirect for migrations)
• Minimum 3 master nodes (recommended: 3 masters + 3 replicas for full HA)

items.label: Setting Up Valkey Cluster
items.code:
# 1. Create directory structure for each node
# You can use any ports you like (7000-7005 in this example).
# Just update the port numbers in the loops below.
user@server:~$ 
mkdir -p ~/cluster-test
cd ~/cluster-test
mkdir 7000 7001 7002 7003 7004 7005

# 2. Generate config files for all 6 nodes using a loop
user@server:~$ 
for port in 7000 7001 7002 7003 7004 7005; do
cat > ~/cluster-test/$port/valkey.conf <<EOF
port $port
bind 0.0.0.0
protected-mode no

cluster-enabled yes
cluster-config-file nodes-$port.conf
cluster-node-timeout 5000

appendonly yes
daemonize no

dir $HOME/cluster-test/$port
EOF
done

# 3. Start all Valkey instances (background with logging)
user@server:~$ 
for port in 7000 7001 7002 7003 7004 7005; do
  nohup valkey-server ~/cluster-test/$port/valkey.conf \
  > ~/cluster-test/$port/valkey.log 2>&1 &
done

# 4. Verify all nodes are listening
user@server:~$ ss -tuln | grep 700

# 5. Create the cluster (3 masters + 3 replicas, 1 replica per master)
user@server:~$ 
valkey-cli --cluster create \
  127.0.0.1:7000 127.0.0.1:7001 127.0.0.1:7002 \
  127.0.0.1:7003 127.0.0.1:7004 127.0.0.1:7005 \
  --cluster-replicas 1

# Output:
>>> Performing hash slots allocation on 6 nodes...
Master[0] -> Slots 0 - 5460
Master[1] -> Slots 5461 - 10922
Master[2] -> Slots 10923 - 16383
Adding replica 127.0.0.1:7004 to 127.0.0.1:7000
Adding replica 127.0.0.1:7005 to 127.0.0.1:7001
Adding replica 127.0.0.1:7003 to 127.0.0.1:7002
>>> Performing Cluster Check (using node 127.0.0.1:7000)
M: abc... 127.0.0.1:7000
   slots:[0-5460] (5461 slots) master
S: def... 127.0.0.1:7004
   replicates abc...
M: ghi... 127.0.0.1:7001
   slots:[5461-10922] (5462 slots) master
...
[OK] All 16384 slots covered

# 6. Connect with cluster mode
user@server:~$ valkey-cli -c -p 7000
127.0.0.1:7000> SET user:1000 "Alice"
-> Redirected to slot [1234] located at 127.0.0.1:7002
OK
127.0.0.1:7002> GET user:1000
"Alice"

items.label: Failover Test
items.text:
Testing automatic failover in Valkey Cluster validates that your cluster is truly resilient. Here's how to simulate a master failure and verify that a replica promotes automatically.

1. **Find the master process for port 7000** — the first master node:
```bash
ps aux | grep valkey | grep 7000
# Note the PID (e.g., 4031)
```

2. **Kill the master process** — simulate a crash:
```bash
kill -9 <PID>
# Replace <PID> with the actual process ID
```

3. **Wait a few seconds** — the cluster detects the failure via gossip protocol and initiates failover. One of the replicas (usually 7004, which was replicating 7000) will promote itself to master.

4. **Verify the promotion** — check which node is now the master for slot range 0-5460:
```bash
valkey-cli -p 7004 cluster nodes
# You should see 127.0.0.1:7004 listed as 'master' with slots assigned
```

That's the power of Valkey Cluster — no manual intervention needed. The cluster self-heals by promoting a replica.
items.code:
# Step 1: Find the master PID for port 7000
user@server:~$ ps aux | grep valkey | grep 7000
user         4031  ...  valkey-server *:7000

# Step 2: Kill the master (simulate crash)
user@server:~$ kill -9 4031

# Step 3: Wait a few seconds (at least cluster-node-timeout) for failover to complete
user@server:~$ sleep 5

# Step 4: Verify promotion — check 7004 (former replica of 7000)
user@server:~$ valkey-cli -p 7004 cluster nodes
# Expected: 127.0.0.1:7004 is now 'master' with slots 0-5460,
# and it no longer shows 'slave' next to its node ID

items.label: Cluster Management Commands
items.code:
# View cluster info
127.0.0.1:7000> CLUSTER INFO
cluster_state:ok
cluster_slots_assigned:16384
cluster_slots_ok:16384
cluster_slots_pfail:0
cluster_slots_fail:0
cluster_known_nodes:6
cluster_size:3
cluster_current_epoch:6
cluster_my_epoch:1
cluster_stats_messages_sent:12345
cluster_stats_messages_received:12345

# View nodes in cluster
127.0.0.1:7000> CLUSTER NODES
aaa... 127.0.0.1:7000@17000 myself,master - 0 0 1 connected 0-5460
bbb... 127.0.0.1:7001@17001 master - 0 1728547200 2 connected 5461-10922
ccc... 127.0.0.1:7002@17002 master - 0 1728547200 3 connected 10923-16383
ddd... 127.0.0.1:7003@17003 slave ccc... 0 1728547200 3 connected
eee... 127.0.0.1:7004@17004 slave aaa... 0 1728547200 1 connected
fff... 127.0.0.1:7005@17005 slave bbb... 0 1728547200 2 connected

# Count keys per node
127.0.0.1:7000> CLUSTER COUNTKEYSINSLOT 0
(integer) 15

# Resharding (move slots to a new node)
user@server:~$ valkey-cli --cluster reshard 127.0.0.1:7000

# Add a new node
user@server:~$ valkey-cli --cluster add-node 127.0.0.1:7006 127.0.0.1:7000

# Add as replica
user@server:~$ valkey-cli --cluster add-node 127.0.0.1:7006 127.0.0.1:7000 --cluster-slave --cluster-master-id <node-id>

# Remove a node
user@server:~$ valkey-cli --cluster del-node 127.0.0.1:7000 <node-id>

# Fix cluster (after partial failures)
user@server:~$ valkey-cli --cluster fix 127.0.0.1:7000

items.label: Cluster Limitations
text: 1. Multi-key operations are limited — You can only operate on keys in the same slot. Use hash tags ({user:1000}.address and {user:1000}.name hash to the same slot) for related keys.
2. No multi-database — Only database 0 is available. SELECT is not supported in cluster mode.
3. Pipelining is per-node — You need separate pipelines for each node, or use client-side aggregation.
4. Lua scripts must target keys in the same slot — All KEYS in EVAL must hash to the same slot.
5. No cross-slot transactions — MULTI/EXEC only works on keys in the same slot.
6. Increased latency — Cross-node queries involve network hops. Keep your data access patterns slot-aware.
7. Client libraries must support cluster mode — Not all Valkey clients handle MOVED/ASK redirects correctly.

sections.title: Valkey Deep Dive
sections.description: Understanding Valkey — its unique features, architecture, and built-in capabilities.

items.label: Why Valkey Exists
items.text:
In March 2024, Valkey Ltd. announced that future versions of Valkey would no longer be open source under the BSD 3-Clause license, adopting instead the Valkey Source Available License (RSAL) and Server Side Public License (SSPL). This change meant that cloud providers and commercial entities could no longer offer Valkey as a managed service without a commercial license.

In response, the Linux Foundation, together with AWS, Google Cloud, Oracle, Ericsson, and other major contributors, forked the last BSD-licensed version (Valkey 7.2.4) to create Valkey — ensuring a permanent, permissively-licensed, community-governed in-memory data store.

items.label: Valkey 9.x Built-in Search
items.code:
# Valkey 9.x introduces built-in full-text and vector search — no external modules needed!
# This is a major differentiator from Valkey OSS which requires the RediSearch module.

# Create a search index on hash keys
127.0.0.1:6379> FT.CREATE idx:products ON HASH PREFIX 1 "product:" SCHEMA \
    name TEXT WEIGHT 5.0 SORTABLE \
    description TEXT WEIGHT 1.0 \
    price NUMERIC SORTABLE \
    category TAG SORTABLE \
    stock NUMERIC SORTABLE
OK

# Add some products
127.0.0.1:6379> HSET product:1 name "Gaming Laptop" description "High-performance laptop" price 1499.99 category "electronics" stock 50
127.0.0.1:6379> HSET product:2 name "Mechanical Keyboard" description "RGB backlit keyboard" price 89.99 category "electronics" stock 200

# Full-text search
127.0.0.1:6379> FT.SEARCH idx:products "laptop" LIMIT 0 10
1) (integer) 1
2) "product:1"
3) 1) "name"
   2) "Gaming Laptop"

# Vector similarity search
127.0.0.1:6379> FT.CREATE idx:vectors ON HASH PREFIX 1 "vec:" SCHEMA embedding VECTOR FLAT 6 TYPE FLOAT32 DIM 384 DISTANCE_METRIC COSINE
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> FT.SEARCH idx:vectors "*=>[KNN 10 @embedding $BLOB AS score]" PARAMS 2 BLOB $vector SORTBY score

items.label: Valkey 8.x+ Enhanced Multithreading
items.text: Valkey 8.x introduced enhanced I/O multithreading that provides near-linear scaling on multi-core systems. This translates to ~8-10% higher throughput and ~20% better P99 latency compared to single-threaded Valkey 7.2.x. Additional features include per-slot metrics for deep performance insight and optimized memory efficiency.

sections.title: Security: ACLs, TLS, Firewall & Hardening
sections.description: Securing your Valkey deployment against unauthorized access and attacks.

items.label: Access Control Lists (ACL)
items.text:
Valkey 6+ (and all Valkey versions) support ACLs, replacing the old single-password model with granular per-user permissions. You can create users with specific command permissions, key patterns, and channel access (Pub/Sub).

ACL rules follow the format: user <username> on/off +<command>|+@<category> -<command> ~<key-pattern> &<channel-pattern> ><password>

items.label: ACL Configuration
items.code:
# ACL: Predefined users in valkey.conf
# =========================================

# Default user (can be disabled or restricted)
user default off -@all +@info +@connection  # Only info & ping

# Application user — can read/write cache:*, but not admin commands
user appuser on +@read +@write +@set -@dangerous +@pubsub ~cache:* ~session:* >app-password-123

# Admin user — full access
user admin on +@all ~* &* >admin-password-456

# Read-only user for analytics
user readonly on +@read ~* >readonly-pass

# Specific command restrictions
user restricted on +@all -@admin -@dangerous -FLUSHALL -FLUSHDB -CONFIG ~* >restricted-pass

# ACL commands (runtime, no restart needed)
127.0.0.1:6379> ACL LIST
1) "user default on nopass ~* &* +@all"
2) "user appuser on #abc... ~cache:* ~session:* +@read +@write -@dangerous"

127.0.0.1:6379> ACL SETUSER developer on >dev-pass +@all ~* &*
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> ACL DELUSER old-user
(integer) 1

127.0.0.1:6379> ACL WHOAMI
"default"

127.0.0.1:6379> AUTH appuser app-password-123
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> ACL WHOAMI
"appuser"

127.0.0.1:6379> ACL GETUSER appuser
1) "flags"
2) 1) "on"
2) "allkeys"  # Actually shows key patterns
...

127.0.0.1:6379> ACL LOG
1) 1) "count"
   2) (integer) 1
   3) "reason"
   4) "command"
   5) "object"
   6) "CONFIG"
   7) "username"
   8) "appuser"

items.label: Step-by-Step ACL User Setup
items.text:
Move beyond single-password auth. Valkey's ACL system provides fine-grained permissions for true "least privilege" security.

The default user has full access with no password — disable it immediately:

```bash
# Lock down the default user
127.0.0.1:6379> ACL SETUSER default off
# Create an Admin user (full access)
127.0.0.1:6379> ACL SETUSER admin on >AdminPass@2024! ~* &* +@all
# Create an App user (least privilege)
127.0.0.1:6379> ACL SETUSER appuser on >AppPass@2024! ~* +@read +@write +@string +@hash +@list +@set +@sortedset +@generic -@dangerous
# Create a Read-Only user (analytics/reporting)
127.0.0.1:6379> ACL SETUSER readonly on >ReadOnly@2024! ~* +@read +@string +@hash +@list +@set
# Create a Monitoring user (health checks only)
127.0.0.1:6379> ACL SETUSER monitor on >Monitor@2024! ~* +ping +info +latency
```

Persist ACLs to survive restarts:
1. Set `aclfile /etc/valkey/users.acl` in valkey.conf
2. Run `ACL SAVE` in valkey-cli

items.label: TLS/SSL Configuration
items.code:
# valkey.conf — Enable TLS
# =============================

port 0                                  # Disable plain TCP

tls-port 6379                           # TLS on default port
tls-cert-file /etc/ssl/certs/redis.crt
tls-key-file /etc/ssl/private/redis.key
tls-ca-cert-file /etc/ssl/certs/ca.crt  # For client cert verification
tls-auth-clients yes                     # Require client certificates
tls-protocols "TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3"
tls-ciphers "HIGH:!aNULL:!eNULL:!EXPORT:!DES:!MD5:!PSK:!RC4"
tls-ciphersuites "TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384:TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256"
tls-prefer-server-ciphers yes

# Replication with TLS
replica-tls-cert-file /etc/ssl/certs/redis.crt
replica-tls-key-file /etc/ssl/private/redis.key
replica-tls-ca-cert-file /etc/ssl/certs/ca.crt
replica-tls-auth-clients yes

# Connect with TLS
user@server:~$ valkey-cli --tls --cert redis.crt --key redis.key --cacert ca.crt -h redis.example.com -p 6379

# Docker with TLS
user@server:~$ docker run -d --name redis-tls \
  -v $(pwd)/ssl:/etc/ssl \
  -v $(pwd)/valkey.conf:/usr/local/etc/valkey/valkey.conf \
  -p 6379:6379 \
  valkey/valkey:9-alpine \
  valkey-server /usr/local/etc/valkey/valkey.conf

items.label: Network Security & Hardening
items.code:
# 1. Bind to localhost or private IP (NOT 0.0.0.0)
# valkey.conf
bind 127.0.0.1 10.0.10.10       # Only localhost + private subnet
protected-mode yes               # Only allow loopback connections if no password set

# 2. Firewall rules (ufw on Ubuntu)
user@server:~$ sudo ufw allow from 10.0.0.0/8 to any port 6379  # Only internal network
user@server:~$ sudo ufw deny 6379                               # Block external access

# 3. Rename dangerous commands (prevent accidental/abusive use)
# valkey.conf
rename-command FLUSHALL ""       # Completely disable
rename-command FLUSHDB ""
rename-command CONFIG "ADMIN_CONFIG"  # Rename to something obscure
rename-command EVAL ""           # Disable if not using Lua
rename-command DEBUG ""
rename-command SHUTDOWN ""
rename-command SLAVEOF ""       # Old name for REPLICAOF

# 4. Set max memory to prevent OOM kills
maxmemory 4gb
maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru     # Eviction policy when memory is full
maxmemory-samples 10             # Accuracy of eviction approximation

# 5. Rate-limit connections
# valkey.conf
timeout 300                       # Close idle connections after 5 minutes
tcp-keepalive 300                 # TCP keepalive interval
maxclients 10000                  # Max simultaneous connected clients

# 6. Disable dangerous commands via ACL
127.0.0.1:6379> ACL SETUSER default on >strong-password +@all -@admin -@dangerous -FLUSHALL -FLUSHDB -CONFIG -DEBUG -SHUTDOWN -SLAVEOF -EVAL

sections.title: Performance Tuning & Memory Optimization
sections.description: Optimizing Valkey for maximum throughput, minimal latency, and efficient memory usage.

items.label: Eviction Policies
items.text:
When Valkey reaches maxmemory, it must evict old data to make room for new writes. The eviction policy determines what gets removed:

• noeviction — Return errors on write operations (not recommended for caching)
• allkeys-lru — Evict the least recently used keys (best for general caching)
• allkeys-lfu — Evict the least frequently used keys (better for hot/cold data patterns)
• volatile-lru — Evict LRU from keys with TTL set (if no TTL keys, falls back to noeviction)
• volatile-lfu — Evict LFU from keys with TTL set
• allkeys-random — Evict random keys (fastest, useful for uniform access)
• volatile-random — Evict random keys from those with TTL
• volatile-ttl — Evict keys with the shortest remaining TTL

Recommendation: Use allkeys-lru for most cache scenarios. Use allkeys-lfu if you have a clear hot/cold access pattern.

items.label: Memory Optimization Techniques
items.code:
# valkey.conf — Memory optimization settings
# ===========================================

# Use jemalloc allocator (default, best for Valkey)
# Check: valkey-cli INFO memory | grep allocator

# Hash encoding optimization (ziplist → hashtable threshold)
hash-max-ziplist-entries 512
hash-max-ziplist-value 64

# List encoding optimization (quicklist)
list-max-ziplist-size -2       # -2 = -8 KB max per node
list-compress-depth 0

# Set encoding optimization (intset for integers)
set-max-intset-entries 512

# Sorted set optimization (ziplist → skiplist threshold)
zset-max-ziplist-entries 128
zset-max-ziplist-value 64

# Active memory defragmentation (Valkey 7+)
activedefrag yes
active-defrag-threshold-lower 10    # Start defrag at 10% fragmentation
active-defrag-threshold-upper 100   # Max defrag intensity at 100%
active-defrag-ignore-bytes 100mb   # Ignore fragmentation below this
active-defrag-cycle-min 1           # Min CPU % for defrag
active-defrag-cycle-max 25          # Max CPU % for defrag
active-defrag-max-scan-fields 1000

# View memory usage
127.0.0.1:6379> INFO memory
# Memory
used_memory:104857600
used_memory_human:100.00M
used_memory_rss:157286400
used_memory_rss_human:150.00M
used_memory_peak:209715200
used_memory_peak_human:200.00M
used_memory_lua:37888
used_memory_scripts:4096
mem_fragmentation_ratio:1.50  # RSS/used — should be close to 1.0
mem_allocator:jemalloc-5.3.0

# Memory usage of individual keys
127.0.0.1:6379> MEMORY USAGE user:1000
(integer) 512   # Bytes used by this key
127.0.0.1:6379> MEMORY USAGE user:1000 SAMPLES 10
(integer) 528   # More accurate with larger sample

# Memory stats
127.0.0.1:6379> MEMORY STATS
 1) "peak.allocated"
 2) (integer) 209715200
 3) "total.allocated"
 4) (integer) 104857600
...

items.label: System-Level Tuning (Linux OS)
items.text:
Valkey performance is heavily dependent on host OS configuration. These three kernel tweaks are essential for production:

**1. Disable Transparent Huge Pages (THP)** — THP causes significant latency spikes and memory fragmentation for Valkey's memory allocator (jemalloc).
```bash
# Check current status
cat /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
# Disable (add to /etc/rc.local or use a systemd unit)
echo never > /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
```

**2. Kernel Overcommit Memory** — Prevents background saving (RDB fork) from failing under memory pressure. The fork() syscall copies page tables and can fail if the system thinks there isn't enough RAM, even though Valkey uses copy-on-write.
```bash
# Add to /etc/sysctl.conf
vm.overcommit_memory = 1
# Apply immediately
sysctl -p
```

**3. TCP Backlog and File Descriptors** — Ensure the OS can handle high connection counts without dropping connections.
```bash
# Increase somaxconn (add to /etc/sysctl.conf)
net.core.somaxconn = 512
# Increase ulimits (add to /etc/security/limits.conf)
valkey soft nofile 65536
valkey hard nofile 65536
```

Without these three tweaks, Valkey will suffer from periodic latency spikes, background save failures under load, and connection drops under high concurrency.

items.label: Observability & Monitoring
items.text:
"If you can't measure it, you can't manage it." Set up a robust monitoring stack before going to production.

**Prometheus & Grafana:** Use the `valkey_exporter` to scrape Valkey metrics. Monitor these key indicators:
• `hit_rate` — Cache effectiveness (keyspace_hits / (keyspace_hits + keyspace_misses))
• `evicted_keys` — Spikes indicate maxmemory is too low or TTLs are too long
• `mem_fragmentation_ratio` — Values > 1.5 indicate memory fragmentation issues
• `connected_clients` — Unexplained growth signals connection leaks
• `repl_lag` — Replication delay in seconds

**Slow Log:** Audit regularly to identify unoptimized queries or large key operations:
```bash
valkey-cli SLOWLOG GET 10
```

**Health Checks:** Implement active monitoring via PING through your orchestration layer (Kubernetes readiness probes, Docker HEALTHCHECK, or external monitoring tools).

items.label: Latency Monitoring & Slow Log
items.code:
# Configure slow log
# valkey.conf
slowlog-log-slower-than 10000   # Log commands slower than 10ms (microseconds)
slowlog-max-len 128             # Keep last 128 slow entries

# View slow log
127.0.0.1:6379> SLOWLOG GET 5
1) 1) (integer) 1                 # Entry ID
   2) (integer) 1728547200        # Unix timestamp
   3) (integer) 45000             # Duration (microseconds) = 45ms
   4) 1) "KEYS"                   # Command
      2) "user:*"                 # Arguments
   5) "127.0.0.1:54321"           # Client IP:port
2) 1) (integer) 0
   2) (integer) 1728547100
   3) (integer) 12000             # 12ms
   4) 1) "SORT"
      2) "large_list"
   5) "127.0.0.1:54322"

# Reset slow log
127.0.0.1:6379> SLOWLOG RESET
OK

# Real-time latency test (built-in)
user@server:~$ valkey-cli --latency
min: 0, max: 3, avg: 0.45 (793 samples)

user@server:~$ valkey-cli --latency-history -i 10
min: 0, max: 2, avg: 0.32 (1479 samples) -- 10.01 seconds range

user@server:~$ valkey-cli --intrinsic-latency 1000
Max latency so far: 1 microseconds.
Max latency so far: 2 microseconds.
Max latency so far: 4 microseconds.
1244556322 total runs (avg latency: 0.8038 microseconds / 803.79 nanoseconds)

# Common causes of high latency:
# - KEYS * or other O(N) commands (use SCAN instead)
# - Large batch operations without pipelining
# - Fork-induced latency (BGSAVE/BGREWRITEAOF) — see below
# - AOF fsync (always — avoid unless critical data)
# - Swap (never let Valkey memory go to swap!)
# - Network congestion or buffer bloat

# Fork latency mitigation (for BGSAVE/BGREWRITEAOF):
# Use replica for BGSAVE (never do it on master)
# or use diskless replication:
repl-diskless-sync yes
# Set vm.overcommit_memory=1 in /etc/sysctl.conf:
user@server:~$ sudo sysctl vm.overcommit_memory=1

items.label: Connection Pooling — Client-Side Best Practices

rows: Use hashes instead of JSON strings
Avoids major latency spikes during save
Faster detection but more false positives
Best balance of safety and speed
Set maxmemory to 70-80% of available RAM
Reduces polling in Pub/Sub and streams

sections.title: Modules & Extensions
sections.description: Extending Valkey with modules like JSON, TimeSeries, Bloom filters.

items.label: Valkey Modules Overview
items.text:
Valkey supports a growing ecosystem of modules that extend its capabilities beyond key-value operations. Since Valkey is wire-compatible with the Valkey RESP protocol, most Valkey modules also work with Valkey:

• RedisJSON — Native JSON document storage with deep path manipulation
• RedisTimeSeries — Time-series data ingestion, aggregation, downsampling
• RedisBloom — Probabilistic data structures (Bloom filters, Cuckoo filters, Count-min sketch, Top-K)

Additionally, Valkey 9.x includes built-in full-text search, vector similarity search, and hybrid search — capabilities that require separate modules in Valkey OSS.

items.label: RedisJSON — Document Store
items.code:
# Store and query JSON documents natively
# Unlike storing JSON strings in regular keys, RedisJSON allows
# per-field operations without fetching/overwriting the entire document.

127.0.0.1:6379> JSON.SET user:1000 $ '{"name":"Alice","age":30,"address":{"city":"NYC","zip":"10001"},"tags":["premium","vip"]}'
OK

# Get entire document
127.0.0.1:6379> JSON.GET user:1000 $
"[{"name":"Alice","age":30,"address":{"city":"NYC","zip":"10001"},"tags":["premium","vip"]}]"

# Get specific fields using JSONPath
127.0.0.1:6379> JSON.GET user:1000 $.name
"[\"Alice\"]"
127.0.0.1:6379> JSON.GET user:1000 $.address.city
"[\"NYC\"]"
127.0.0.1:6379> JSON.GET user:1000 $.tags[0]
"[\"premium\"]"

# Update individual fields
127.0.0.1:6379> JSON.SET user:1000 $.age 31
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> JSON.ARRAPPEND user:1000 $.tags "new-york"
1) (integer) 3    # New array length

# Increment numeric field
127.0.0.1:6379> JSON.NUMINCRBY user:1000 $.age 1
"32"

# Delete fields
127.0.0.1:6379> JSON.DEL user:1000 $.address.zip
(integer) 1

# Check if field exists
127.0.0.1:6379> JSON.TYPE user:1000 $.name
1) "string"
127.0.0.1:6379> JSON.TYPE user:1000 $.tags
1) "array"

items.label: RediSearch — Full-Text & Vector Search
items.code:
# Create a search index on hash keys
127.0.0.1:6379> FT.CREATE idx:products ON HASH PREFIX 1 "product:" SCHEMA \
    name TEXT WEIGHT 5.0 SORTABLE \
    description TEXT WEIGHT 1.0 \
    price NUMERIC SORTABLE \
    category TAG SORTABLE \
    stock NUMERIC SORTABLE
OK

# Add some products
127.0.0.1:6379> HSET product:1 name "Gaming Laptop" description "High-performance laptop with RTX 4080" price 1499.99 category "electronics" stock 50
127.0.0.1:6379> HSET product:2 name "Mechanical Keyboard" description "RGB backlit mechanical keyboard" price 89.99 category "electronics" stock 200
127.0.0.1:6379> HSET product:3 name "Ergonomic Mouse" description "Wireless vertical ergonomic mouse" price 49.99 category "electronics" stock 150

# Full-text search
127.0.0.1:6379> FT.SEARCH idx:products "laptop" LIMIT 0 10
1) (integer) 1
2) "product:1"
3) 1) "name"
   2) "Gaming Laptop"

# Search with filters
127.0.0.1:6379> FT.SEARCH idx:products "mechanical" FILTER price 50 200 SORTBY price DESC
1) (integer) 1
2) "product:2"

# Faceted search (aggregation)
127.0.0.1:6379> FT.AGGREGATE idx:products "*" GROUPBY 1 @category REDUCE COUNT 0 AS count
1) (integer) 1
2) 1) "category"
   2) "electronics"
   3) "count"
   4) "3"

# Vector similarity search (Valkey 8.0+)
# FT.CREATE idx:vectors ON HASH PREFIX 1 "vec:" SCHEMA embedding VECTOR FLAT 6 TYPE FLOAT32 DIM 384 DISTANCE_METRIC COSINE
# FT.SEARCH idx:vectors "*=>[KNN 10 @embedding $BLOB AS score]" PARAMS 2 BLOB $vector SORTBY score

items.label: RedisTimeSeries & RedisBloom
items.code:
# RedisTimeSeries — Time-series data
# ======================================

# Create a time series
127.0.0.1:6379> TS.CREATE sensor:temperature:room1 RETENTION 86400000 LABELS type "temperature" room "room1"
OK

# Add data points (timestamp in milliseconds)
127.0.0.1:6379> TS.ADD sensor:temperature:room1 1728547200000 22.5
(integer) 1728547200000
127.0.0.1:6379> TS.ADD sensor:temperature:room1 1728547260000 23.1
127.0.0.1:6379> TS.ADD sensor:temperature:room1 1728547320000 22.8

# Range query with aggregation
127.0.0.1:6379> TS.RANGE sensor:temperature:room1 1728547200000 1728547400000 AGGREGATION avg 60000
1) 1) 1728547200000
   2) 22.5
2) 1) 1728547260000
   2) 23.1
3) 1) 1728547320000
   2) 22.8

# Get last sample
127.0.0.1:6379> TS.GET sensor:temperature:room1
1) 1728547320000
2) 22.8

# RedisBloom — Probabilistic data structures
# =============================================

# Bloom filter — check if item EXISTS (may have false positives, NO false negatives)
127.0.0.1:6379> BF.RESERVE seen:usernames 0.01 1000000  # 1% error rate, 1M capacity
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> BF.ADD seen:usernames "alice"
(integer) 1
127.0.0.1:6379> BF.EXISTS seen:usernames "alice"
(integer) 1  # Definitely exists
127.0.0.1:6379> BF.EXISTS seen:usernames "mallory"
(integer) 0  # Definitely does NOT exist (might be false positive)

# Cuckoo filter — Bloom filter with deletion support
127.0.0.1:6379> CF.RESERVE emails 1000000
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> CF.ADDNX emails "user@example.com"
(integer) 1
127.0.0.1:6379> CF.DEL emails "user@example.com"
(integer) 1

# Top-K — track most frequent items
127.0.0.1:6379> TOPK.RESERVE trending:hashtags 10  # Track top 10
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> TOPK.ADD trending:hashtags "#redis" "#database" "#performance" "#redis" "#redis" "#tutorial"
1) (nil)
2) (nil)
127.0.0.1:6379> TOPK.LIST trending:hashtags
1) "#redis"
2) "#tutorial"
3) "#database"
4) "#performance"

sections.title: Use Cases & Design Patterns
sections.description: Real-world use cases with production-ready design patterns for Valkey.

items.label: 1. Caching Layer
items.text:
Valkey as a cache is the most common use case. It sits between your application and database, storing frequently accessed data in memory for sub-millisecond retrieval.

Pattern: Cache-Aside (Lazy Loading)
1. Application checks cache (GET key)
2. If found (cache hit), return data
3. If not found (cache miss), query database, store in cache (SETEX), return data
4. On writes, invalidate or update cache

items.label: Caching Implementation
items.code:
# Cache-aside pattern with TTL
# =================================

# Application code (Python example)
import redis
import json

r = redis.Valkey(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)

def get_user(user_id):
    cache_key = f"user:{user_id}:profile"

    # 1. Try cache
    cached = r.get(cache_key)
    if cached:
        return json.loads(cached)

    # 2. Cache miss — query database (simulated)
    user = query_database(user_id)
    if not user:
        return None

    # 3. Store in cache for 1 hour
    r.setex(cache_key, 3600, json.dumps(user))
    return user

# Invalidation on update
def update_user(user_id, data):
    # Update database
    update_database(user_id, data)

    # Invalidate cache (or update it)
    cache_key = f"user:{user_id}:profile"
    r.delete(cache_key)  # Next read will fetch fresh data

# Caching best practices:
# - TTL every cached item (prevents stale data)
# - Use consistent key naming: resource:type:id
# - Invalidate on writes — don't try to keep cache in sync
# - Cache the query result, not the raw data
# - Monitor cache hit ratio: INFO stats -> keyspace_hits / (keyspace_hits + keyspace_misses)

items.label: 2. Session Store
items.code:
# Store user sessions with automatic expiration
# ================================================

# Store session on login
127.0.0.1:6379> HSET session:abc123 user_id 42 ip "192.168.1.1" created_at "2026-06-08T10:00:00Z" role "admin"
127.0.0.1:6379> EXPIRE session:abc123 86400   # 24-hour session

# Verify session on each request
127.0.0.1:6379> TTL session:abc123
(integer) 82800  # Still valid
127.0.0.1:6379> HGET session:abc123 user_id
"42"

# Extend session on activity
127.0.0.1:6379> EXPIRE session:abc123 86400   # Reset 24h timer

# Logout — delete session
127.0.0.1:6379> DEL session:abc123

# List all active sessions (SCAN for production!)
user@server:~$ valkey-cli SCAN 0 MATCH session:* COUNT 1000
1) "0"
2) 1) "session:abc123"
   2) "session:def456"

items.label: 3. Rate Limiting
items.code:
