# Sliding window rate limiter using Sorted Sets
# ================================================

# Pattern: Allow N requests per M seconds per user/IP
# Uses ZREMRANGEBYSCORE to remove old entries, then ZCARD to count

# Lua script for atomic rate limiting
127.0.0.1:6379> EVAL "
    local key = KEYS[1]
    local limit = tonumber(ARGV[1])      -- Max requests
    local window = tonumber(ARGV[2])     -- Time window (seconds)
    local now = tonumber(ARGV[3])
    local window_start = now - (window * 1000)  -- Convert to milliseconds

    -- Remove old entries
    redis.call('ZREMRANGEBYSCORE', key, 0, window_start)

    -- Count remaining entries
    local current = redis.call('ZCARD', key)

    if current >= limit then
        return -1  -- Rate limit exceeded
    end

    -- Add current request
    redis.call('ZADD', key, now, now)
    redis.call('EXPIRE', key, window)
    return limit - current - 1  -- Remaining requests
" 1 rate:api:user:42 100 60 1728547200000

# Token bucket algorithm (using strings with INCR + EXPIRE)
# Allow 10 requests per minute, but allows bursting
127.0.0.1:6379> EVAL "
    local key = KEYS[1]
    local limit = tonumber(ARGV[1])      -- Max requests per window
    local window = tonumber(ARGV[2])     -- Window in seconds
    local now = tonumber(ARGV[3])
    local window_key = key .. ':' .. math.floor(now / (window * 1000))

    local count = redis.call('INCR', window_key)
    if count == 1 then
        redis.call('EXPIRE', window_key, window * 2)
    end

    if count > limit then
        return -1  -- Rate limited
    end
    return limit - count  -- Remaining
" 1 rate:api 100 60 1728547200000

# Generic Cell Rate Algorithm (GCRA) — most precise
# Use redis-cell module or implement via Lua

items.label: 4. Message Queue with Streams
items.code:
# Producer — add tasks to stream
127.0.0.1:6379> XADD tasks:email * to "user@example.com" subject "Welcome!" template "welcome-email"
"1728547200000-0"
127.0.0.1:6379> XADD tasks:email * to "admin@example.com" subject "Alert: Server Down" template "alert"
"1728547200123-0"

# Create consumer group
127.0.0.1:6379> XGROUP CREATE tasks:email email-workers $ MKSTREAM
OK

# Consumer — read and process
127.0.0.1:6379> XREADGROUP GROUP email-workers worker-1 COUNT 1 BLOCK 5000 STREAMS tasks:email >
1) 1) "tasks:email"
   2) 1) 1) "1728547200000-0"
         2) 1) "to"
            2) "user@example.com"
            3) "subject"
            4) "Welcome!"
            5) "template"
            6) "welcome-email"

# Process the email... then acknowledge
127.0.0.1:6379> XACK tasks:email email-workers "1728547200000-0"
(integer) 1

# Check pending messages
127.0.0.1:6379> XPENDING tasks:email email-workers
1) (integer) 1  # One unacknowledged message
2) "1728547200123-0"
3) "1728547200123-0"
4) 1) 1) "worker-1"
      2) "1"

127.0.0.1:6379> PFCOUNT stats:uv:2026-06-08
(integer) 3  # Approximate unique visitors

items.label: 6. Distributed Locking (Redlock)
items.code:
# Simple distributed lock with SET NX + TTL
127.0.0.1:6379> SET resource:lock:order-123 "server-1" NX EX 30
OK  # Lock acquired
127.0.0.1:6379> SET resource:lock:order-123 "server-2" NX EX 30
(nil)  # Lock NOT acquired (already held)

# Release lock (Lua script — must be atomic!)
127.0.0.1:6379> EVAL "
    if redis.call('GET', KEYS[1]) == ARGV[1] then
        return redis.call('DEL', KEYS[1])
    else
        return 0
    end
" 1 resource:lock:order-123 "server-1"

# Redlock algorithm (for multi-master Valkey, e.g., 5 independent nodes):
# 1. Get current timestamp
# 2. Try to acquire lock on all N nodes (with short timeout)
# 3. If majority (N/2 + 1) succeed AND elapsed time < lock TTL → lock acquired
# 4. If lock not acquired, release from ALL nodes
#
# Implementation exists in Redisson (Java), redis-py, and other clients.

sections.title: Production Deployment
sections.description: Docker, monitoring, backups, and operational best practices for Valkey.

items.label: Docker Compose — Full Stack
items.code:
# docker-compose.yml — Valkey + Sentinel + App
version: '3.8'

services:
  valkey-master:
    image: valkey/valkey:9-alpine
    container_name: redis-master
    command: valkey-server /usr/local/etc/valkey/valkey.conf
    volumes:
      - ./valkey-master.conf:/usr/local/etc/valkey/valkey.conf
      - valkey-master-data:/data
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
    networks:
      - valkey-net
    restart: unless-stopped
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "valkey-cli", "ping"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 3

  valkey-replica-1:
    image: valkey/valkey:9-alpine
    container_name: redis-replica-1
    command: valkey-server /usr/local/etc/valkey/valkey.conf --replicaof redis-master 6379
    volumes:
      - ./valkey-replica.conf:/usr/local/etc/valkey/valkey.conf
      - valkey-replica-1-data:/data
    depends_on:
      - redis-master
    networks:
      - valkey-net
    restart: unless-stopped

  valkey-sentinel-1:
    image: valkey/valkey:9-alpine
    container_name: valkey-sentinel-1
    command: valkey-sentinel /usr/local/etc/valkey/sentinel.conf
    volumes:
      - ./sentinel.conf:/usr/local/etc/valkey/sentinel.conf
    depends_on:
      - redis-master
    networks:
      - valkey-net
    restart: unless-stopped

  app:
    build: ./app
    container_name: app
    environment:
      - REDIS_HOST=redis-master
      - REDIS_PORT=6379
      - REDIS_SENTINEL_HOSTS=valkey-sentinel-1 26379,valkey-sentinel-2 26379,valkey-sentinel-3 26379
      - REDIS_MASTER_NAME=mymaster
    depends_on:
      - redis-master
    networks:
      - valkey-net
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"

volumes:
  valkey-master-data:
  valkey-replica-1-data:

networks:
  valkey-net:
    driver: bridge

items.label: Production valkey.conf Template
items.code:
# Production-ready valkey.conf — organized by concern
# =====================================================

# --- NETWORK ---
# bind 127.0.0.1              # Localhost only (Secure Default)
# bind 10.0.0.5               # Specific Private IP (Recommended)
port 6379
protected-mode yes
tcp-backlog 511
timeout 300                    # Close idle connections after 5 min
tcp-keepalive 60

# --- ACL / AUTH ---
aclfile /etc/valkey/users.acl

# --- MEMORY MANAGEMENT ---
maxmemory 512mb                # ALWAYS set this!
maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru   # Evict least recently used keys when full
maxmemory-samples 5

# --- PERSISTENCE ---
save 900 1
save 300 10
save 60 10000
dbfilename dump.rdb
dir /var/lib/valkey

appendonly yes
appendfilename "appendonly.aof"
appendfsync everysec
aof-use-rdb-preamble yes
auto-aof-rewrite-percentage 100
auto-aof-rewrite-min-size 64mb

# --- LOGGING ---
loglevel notice
logfile /var/log/valkey/valkey.log
slowlog-log-slower-than 10000
slowlog-max-len 128

# --- SECURITY — Disable/Rename Dangerous Commands ---
rename-command FLUSHALL ""
rename-command FLUSHDB  ""
rename-command DEBUG    ""
rename-command CONFIG   "CONFIG_RESTRICTED_9f3kd"
rename-command SHUTDOWN "SHUTDOWN_RESTRICTED_8x2pq"

items.label: Kubernetes with Valkey Operator
items.code:
# Using the Valkey Operator for K8s
# Installation:
user@k8s:~$ helm repo add ot-helm https://ot-container-kit.github.io/helm-charts/
user@k8s:~$ helm install redis-operator ot-helm/redis-operator

# Create a Valkey cluster
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: redis.redis.opstreelabs.in/v1beta1
kind: RedisCluster
metadata:
  name: redis-cluster
spec:
  clusterSize: 3  # Number of master nodes
  podSecurityContext:
    runAsUser: 1000
    fsGroup: 1000
  kubernetesConfig:
    image: valkey/valkey:9-alpine
    imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: 1Gi
        cpu: 500m
      limits:
        memory: 2Gi
        cpu: 1000m
  storage:
    volumeClaimTemplate:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 10Gi
EOF

# Expose via service
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: redis-service
spec:
  selector:
    app: redis-cluster
  ports:
    - port: 6379
      targetPort: 6379
  type: ClusterIP

  - job_name: 'redis'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['localhost:9121']

# Grafana dashboard: https://grafana.com/grafana/dashboards/11835 (Valkey Dashboard)
# Import via Grafana UI -> Import -> 11835

# Key metrics to monitor:
# - valkey_memory_used_bytes / maxmemory (memory pressure)
# - valkey_connected_clients (connection leak detection)
# - valkey_instantaneous_ops_per_sec (throughput)
# - valkey_keyspace_hits / valkey_keyspace_misses (cache hit ratio)
# - valkey_master_repl_offset / valkey_slave_repl_offset (replication lag)
# - valkey_rdb_bgsave_in_progress (fork latency)
# - valkey_slowlog_length (slow queries)

items.label: Operational Cheat Sheet
items.code:
# Quick reference for day-to-day operations
# ============================================

# Check server health
user@server:~$ valkey-cli INFO server
user@server:~$ valkey-cli INFO stats
user@server:~$ valkey-cli PING

# Check memory
user@server:~$ valkey-cli INFO memory | grep -E "used_memory_human|maxmemory|mem_fragmentation_ratio"

# Check replication
user@server:~$ valkey-cli INFO replication

# Check slow log
user@server:~$ valkey-cli SLOWLOG GET 5

# Check connected clients
user@server:~$ valkey-cli CLIENT LIST
user@server:~$ valkey-cli CLIENT KILL <ip>:<port>  # Kill a specific client

# Flush database (DANGER!)
user@server:~$ valkey-cli FLUSHDB     # Flush current DB
user@server:~$ valkey-cli FLUSHALL    # Flush ALL databases

# Config changes at runtime
user@server:~$ valkey-cli CONFIG SET maxmemory 2gb
user@server:~$ valkey-cli CONFIG SET maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru
user@server:~$ valkey-cli CONFIG REWRITE  # Persist to valkey.conf

# Backup
user@server:~$ valkey-cli BGSAVE
user@server:~$ sudo cp /var/lib/valkey/dump.rdb /backup/valkey_$(date +%F).rdb

# Restart
user@server:~$ sudo systemctl restart valkey

# Shutdown gracefully
user@server:~$ valkey-cli SHUTDOWN  # Saves RDB, flushes AOF, stops cleanly
user@server:~$ valkey-cli SHUTDOWN NOSAVE  # No persistence, immediate stop

sections.title: Troubleshooting Common Issues
sections.description: Diagnosing and fixing the most common Valkey problems.

rows: valkey-cli: Could not connect to Valkey at 127.0.0.1:6379: Connection refused
Valkey not running, wrong port, binding to wrong interface
sudo systemctl status valkey; check bind directive; check port
MISCONF Valkey is configured to save RDB snapshots, but is currently not able to persist on disk
Check disk space; fix /var/lib/valkey permissions; config set stop-writes-on-bgsave-error no (temporary)
OOM command not allowed when used memory > 'maxmemory'
maxmemory reached, eviction policy preventing writes
Increase maxmemory; change eviction policy; identify and remove unnecessary keys
BGSAVE fork, AOF rewrite fork, swap usage
Disable THP; set vm.overcommit_memory=1; use replica for BGSAVE; increase maxmemory headroom
INFO replication shows large offset difference
Network latency, master overload, backlog too small
Increase repl-backlog-size; check network; add replicas for read offload
Check requirepass; check ACL LIST; verify user permissions
KEYS *, LRANGE large lists, ZRANGEBYSCORE on huge ZSET
Replace KEYS with SCAN; trim lists; use pagination; add indexes
Enable activedefrag; restart to defrag; use jemalloc (default)
Quorum not reached, replica not ready
Check sentinel config; ensure odd number of sentinels; verify replica lag is low
Node failure caused unassigned slots, resharding in progress
CLUSTER FIX; add replicas; check node health; reshard manually

items.label: Debugging Toolkit
items.code:
# 1. Real-time command monitoring
user@server:~$ valkey-cli MONITOR
# WARNING: Use only for debugging — MONITOR reduces throughput by ~50%!

# 2. Slow log analysis
user@server:~$ valkey-cli SLOWLOG GET 20 | python -c "
import sys
for line in sys.stdin:
    print(line.strip())
"

# 3. Client list
user@server:~$ valkey-cli CLIENT LIST
id=1234 addr=192.168.1.42:54321 fd=8 name= age=342 idle=0 flags=N db=0 sub=0 psub=0 multi=-1 qbuf=0 qbuf-free=0 obl=0 oll=0 omem=0 events=r cmd=GET

# 4. Key space analysis
user@server:~$ valkey-cli --bigkeys  # Find largest keys

# 5. Memory usage by key
user@server:~$ valkey-cli --memkeys   # Find keys using most memory

# 6. Scan for key patterns
user@server:~$ valkey-cli --scan --pattern "user:*" | head -100

# 7. Latency test
user@server:~$ valkey-cli --latency -h remote-server -p 6379

# 8. Check for THP (Transparent Huge Pages)
user@server:~$ cat /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabledmm/transparent_hugepage/enabled
[always] madvise never  # If 'always', DISABLE IT!
user@server:~$ echo never | sudo tee /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled

# 9. Check overcommit memory
user@server:~$ sysctl vm.overcommit_memory
vm.overcommit_memory = 1  # Should be 1 for Valkey

# 10. Max open files
user@server:~$ cat /proc/$(pgrep valkey-server)/limits | grep "open files"
Max open files            65536                65536                files

# 11. Check swap usage (Valkey should NEVER swap!)
user@server:~$ cat /proc/$(pgrep valkey-server)/status | grep -i swap
VmSwap:       0 kB  # Should be 0

items.label: Valkey/Valkey Configuration Reference
items.code:
# Essential production valkey.conf settings
# ============================================

# Network
bind 127.0.0.1 10.0.10.10
port 6379
tcp-backlog 511
timeout 0
tcp-keepalive 300

# General
daemonize no
supervised systemd
pidfile /var/run/valkey_6379.pid
loglevel notice
logfile /var/log/valkey/redis.log
databases 16
always-show-logo no

# Snapshots (RDB)
save 900 1
save 300 10
save 60 10000
stop-writes-on-bgsave-error yes
rdbcompression yes
rdbchecksum yes
dbfilename dump.rdb
dir /var/lib/valkey

# Replication
replica-serve-stale-data yes
replica-read-only yes
repl-diskless-sync yes
repl-diskless-sync-delay 5
repl-backlog-size 100mb
replica-priority 100
min-replicas-to-write 1
min-replicas-max-lag 10

# Security
requirepass your-strong-password
rename-command FLUSHALL ""
rename-command FLUSHDB ""
rename-command DEBUG ""
rename-command CONFIG "ADMIN_CONFIG"

# Memory
maxmemory 4gb
maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru
maxmemory-samples 10

# AOF
appendonly yes
appendfilename "appendonly.aof"
appendfsync everysec
no-appendfsync-on-rewrite no
auto-aof-rewrite-percentage 100
auto-aof-rewrite-min-size 128mb
aof-load-truncated yes
aof-use-rdb-preamble yes

# Lua
lua-time-limit 5000

# Slow log
slowlog-log-slower-than 10000
slowlog-max-len 128

# Latency monitor
latency-monitor-threshold 0

# Notifications
notify-keyspace-events ""

# Advanced
hash-max-ziplist-entries 512
hash-max-ziplist-value 64
list-max-ziplist-size -2
list-compress-depth 0
set-max-intset-entries 512
zset-max-ziplist-entries 128
zset-max-ziplist-value 64
activerehashing yes
client-output-buffer-limit normal 0 0 0
client-output-buffer-limit replica 256mb 64mb 60
client-output-buffer-limit pubsub 32mb 8mb 60
hz 10
dynamic-hz yes
aof-rewrite-incremental-fsync yes
rdb-save-incremental-fsync yes
activedefrag yes

sections.title: Anti-Patterns & Common Mistakes
sections.description: Frequent pitfalls and anti-patterns that can degrade Valkey performance, reliability, and security — and how to avoid them.

items.label: Using KEYS * in Production
items.text:
KEYS * blocks the Valkey event loop for the entire duration of the scan. On a dataset with millions of keys, this can block all operations for seconds or even minutes — causing timeouts, cascading failures, and Sentinel failovers.

✅ Fix: Always use SCAN (cursor-based iteration) instead:
SCAN 0 MATCH user:* COUNT 100

In Valkey 7.4+, you can also configure the max iterations for SCAN.

items.label: Storing Massive Blobs / Large Keys
items.text:
Storing multi-megabyte values in Valkey causes:
• Network congestion — transferring large payloads blocks the connection
• Latency spikes — serialization/deserialization of large objects is slow
• Memory fragmentation — allocating large blocks stresses jemalloc
• Blocked replicas — replication streams large values synchronously

✅ Fix: Break large objects into hashes (individual fields for each attribute). Valkey is optimized for many small keys, not few large ones. The recommended max value size is 10-100 KB per key.

items.label: Using Valkey as the Primary Database
items.text:
Valkey is an in-memory store — if you lose the server (crash, OOM kill, hardware failure), you lose data. Even with AOF+RDB persistence, the recovery window means data loss is possible.

✅ Fix: Use Valkey as a cache layer in front of a durable database (PostgreSQL, MySQL, etc.). Write to the database first, then populate Valkey. Never treat Valkey as your source of truth unless you fully understand the persistence and replication tradeoffs.

items.label: Shared Instances (Noisy Neighbor)
items.text:
Using a single Valkey instance for multiple microservices creates "noisy neighbor" problems — one service's heavy load (large scans, pipelining bursts, slow queries) impacts all other services sharing the same instance.

✅ Fix: Use separate Valkey instances per service (or per functional domain). Use RESP3 client-set-name and monitoring to identify which service is causing load. In containerized environments, allocate dedicated instances per microservice.

items.label: Ignoring TTL — Memory Leaks
items.text:
Storing data in Valkey without setting TTLs is the #1 cause of out-of-memory errors. Every key that doesn't expire stays in memory forever — until maxmemory is reached and Valkey starts evicting (or rejecting writes).

✅ Fix: Every cached item should have a TTL. Use SETEX or explicitly call EXPIRE. Monitor total keys with INFO keyspace and set up alerts for unexpected key count growth. Use maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru as a safety net, but don't rely on it as your primary cleanup mechanism.

items.label: Blocking Operations on the Main Thread
items.text:
Several operations block the Valkey main thread, preventing ALL other clients from executing commands:
• KEYS * — O(N) scan
• SMEMBERS on large sets — returns ALL members
• FLUSHALL / FLUSHDB (sync) — deletes everything
• SORT on large lists/sets
• ZRANGEBYSCORE without LIMIT
• BRPOPLPUSH with large data
• MONITOR — reduces throughput by ~50%

✅ Fix: Use SCAN family (SCAN, SSCAN, HSCAN, ZSCAN) for iteration. Use UNLINK instead of DEL for large keys (async deletion). Use FLUSHALL ASYNC. Always add LIMIT to range queries.

rows: Valkey = cache, DB = source of truth
No auth, no limits, no persistence

sections.title: Summary & Best Practices
sections.description: Key takeaways, production checklist, and next steps for mastering Valkey/Valkey.

items.label: Key Takeaways
text: Valkey is an in-memory data structure store — not just a cache. It supports strings, lists, sets, sorted sets, hashes, streams, bitmaps, HyperLogLog, and geospatial indices.

Valkey 8.0 introduces the INCREX command (atomic counter + expiry), ARRAY data structure (efficient indexed lists), and Hash Field Expiration (per-field TTLs). Valkey also returned to AGPLv3 open-source licensing in 2025.
Valkey is the open-source fork of Valkey under BSD 3-Clause license, governed by the Linux Foundation. It's a drop-in replacement with added search capabilities in version 9.x.
Choose your data type wisely — Hashes for objects, Sorted Sets for leaderboards, Streams for message queues, Bitmaps for boolean tracking, HyperLogLog for unique counting.
Persistence is configurable — Use hybrid (RDB + AOF) for production, RDB-only for cache, AOF-everysec for critical data, or disable entirely for pure cache.
Replication + Sentinel provides high availability — 1 master, 2+ replicas, 3+ Sentinels for automatic failover.
Valkey Cluster shards data across nodes using 16,384 hash slots — ideal for datasets larger than single-node RAM.
ACLs replace the old single-password model — create users with specific command and key permissions.
Avoid KEYS *, FLUSHALL, and other dangerous commands in production — rename or disable them in valkey.conf.
Monitor performance with SLOWLOG, INFO commandstats, latency-monitor-threshold, and Prometheus/Grafana.
Design for failure — test failover scenarios, practice disaster recovery, and always have a backup strategy.

items.label: Production Readiness Checklist
text: ☐ ACLs implemented (No default user access) — Use ACL SETUSER, not just requirepass
☐ ACL file persisted to disk — Set aclfile in valkey.conf and run ACL SAVE
☐ Bind to specific interfaces — Never 0.0.0.0 in production
☐ Configure protected-mode yes
☐ Rename or disable dangerous commands (FLUSHALL, FLUSHDB, CONFIG, DEBUG, SHUTDOWN)
☐ Set maxmemory with appropriate eviction policy
☐ Enable persistence (hybrid RDB + AOF recommended)
☐ Set up replication with at least 2 replicas
☐ Configure Sentinel (3+ instances) for automatic failover
☐ Set up Prometheus valkey_exporter + Grafana monitoring
☐ Configure slowlog-log-slower-than and monitor regularly
☐ Set up automated backups (RDB snapshots) with retention policy
☐ Test backup restoration procedure
☐ TLS/SSL enabled (for remote/cloud connections)
☐ Disable Transparent Huge Pages (THP) on the host OS
☐ Set vm.overcommit_memory=1 in sysctl
☐ Increase max open files (ulimit -n 65536) for high-traffic deployments
☐ Use connection pooling in application clients
☐ Implement circuit breakers for Valkey connection failures
☐ Document key naming conventions (app:module:entity:id:field)
☐ Set up alerts for: replication lag, memory pressure, OOM errors, failover events
☐ Review SLOWLOG weekly — identify and optimize slow queries
☐ Conduct failover drills quarterly
☐ Test failover (Manual test of Sentinel/Cluster)
☐ Keep Valkey version up to date (subscribe to security advisories)

items.label: Next Steps
text: 1. Install Valkey or Valkey locally and work through every command in this guide — hands-on practice is the best teacher.
2. Build a caching layer — Add Valkey caching to an existing application. Monitor the cache hit ratio improvement.
3. Implement a real-time leaderboard — Use Sorted Sets for a game or application that needs ranked data.
4. Set up Valkey Sentinel — Configure 3 Sentinels and practice killing the master to test automatic failover.
5. Deploy Valkey Cluster — Start with 6 nodes (3 masters + 3 replicas) and practice resharding.
6. Migrate to Valkey — Test Valkey 9.x as a drop-in replacement and explore its built-in search features.
7. Monitor and optimize — Set up Prometheus/Grafana monitoring. Tune the slow log, eviction policy, and memory settings based on real metrics.
8. Explore Redis Stack — Add RedisJSON for document storage and RediSearch for full-text search in your application.
9. Implement a message queue — Use Valkey Streams with consumer groups to build a reliable task processing pipeline.
10. Go deeper — Read the Valkey documentation at redis.io/docs, explore the Valkey project at valkey.io, and study the source code to understand the internals.
